rules.title: Creation of Agency
rules.prompt_question: How is an agency relationship created?
rules.rule_statement: Agency is the fiduciary relationship that arises when a principal manifests assent that an agent shall act on the principal's behalf and subject to the principal's control, and the agent manifests assent or otherwise consents so to act.
rules.application_example: A business owner hires a manager to enter contracts for the store subject to the owner's oversight.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Agency can exist even without a formal contract or payment if the elements of assent, benefit, and control are present.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the principal manifested assent for another person to act on the principal's behalf.
Then ask whether the alleged agent consented to act and whether the principal had the right to control the agent's conduct.
Use the facts to show acting on behalf of the principal, not just cooperation between two people.
Apply the relationship facts carefully. A business owner hires a manager to enter contracts for the store subject to the owner's oversight.
Conclude whether an agency relationship existed at the time of the disputed act.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Agency can exist even without a formal contract or payment if the elements of assent, benefit, and control are present.

rules.exam_tip: On an MEE agency issue, write the three core elements clearly: assent, acting on behalf of the principal, and control. Then attach each element to a fact.

rules.title: Types of Principals
rules.prompt_question: What are the types of principals in agency law?
rules.rule_statement: A principal may be disclosed, partially disclosed, or undisclosed. A disclosed principal is known to the third party and the third party knows the agent acts for a principal. A partially disclosed principal is known to exist but not identified. An undisclosed principal is not known to exist.
rules.application_example: An agent signs a contract without revealing the principal's identity, creating a partially disclosed principal situation.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The principal's disclosure status affects the liability of the principal and agent on contracts.
how_to_apply: Ask what the third party knew at the time of contracting.
Decide whether the third party knew both that a principal existed and who the principal was.
Classify the principal as disclosed, partially disclosed, or undisclosed based on that knowledge.
Then connect that classification to contract liability for both the principal and the agent.
Conclude by stating who can be held liable on the contract and why.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The principal's disclosure status affects the liability of the principal and agent on contracts.

rules.exam_tip: When principal status is tested, classify the principal based on what the third party knew at the time of contracting. That classification usually controls who is liable on the contract.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Express Actual Authority
rules.prompt_question: When does an agent have express actual authority?
rules.rule_statement: An agent has express actual authority when the principal expressly communicates to the agent authority to act on the principal's behalf.
rules.application_example: A company expressly tells its purchasing agent to buy 100 computers.
rules.common_trap: Wrong perspective: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent. Blending authority doctrines: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.
how_to_apply: Start with communications from the principal to the agent, not to the third party.
Identify the exact instruction, permission, or authorization the principal gave the agent.
Compare the act taken by the agent to the scope of that express authorization.
If the act exceeded the instruction, explain precisely where authority stopped.
Conclude whether the agent had express actual authority for this specific act.

common_traps.title: Wrong perspective
common_traps.explanation: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent.

common_traps.title: Blending authority doctrines
common_traps.explanation: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.

rules.exam_tip: For actual authority, start with communications between the principal and agent. Do not use the third party's belief unless you are separately analyzing apparent authority.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Implied Actual Authority
rules.prompt_question: When does an agent have implied actual authority?
rules.rule_statement: An agent has implied actual authority to do acts reasonably necessary to accomplish an expressly authorized task or acts customarily associated with the agent's position.
rules.application_example: A store manager expressly authorized to run the store has implied authority to order routine supplies.
rules.common_trap: Wrong perspective: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent. Blending authority doctrines: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.
how_to_apply: Begin with the express authority or position the agent already had.
Ask whether the disputed act was reasonably necessary to carry out that authority or was customary for that role.
Use surrounding facts such as past practice, industry custom, job title, and ordinary business needs.
Distinguish acts that are incidental from acts that are extraordinary or forbidden.
Conclude whether implied actual authority extended to the act in question.

common_traps.title: Wrong perspective
common_traps.explanation: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent.

common_traps.title: Blending authority doctrines
common_traps.explanation: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.

rules.exam_tip: For actual authority, start with communications between the principal and agent. Do not use the third party's belief unless you are separately analyzing apparent authority.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Apparent Authority
rules.prompt_question: When does apparent authority arise?
rules.rule_statement: Apparent authority arises when a principal's manifestations to a third party would cause a reasonable third party to believe that the agent is authorized to act on the principal's behalf.
rules.application_example: A company gives an employee the title 'Vice President of Sales,' causing customers reasonably to believe the employee can enter ordinary sales contracts.
rules.common_trap: Using the agent's statements: The classic mistake is relying on the agent's own claim of authority. Apparent authority must come from the principal's manifestations to the third party. Skipping reliance: Even if the agent looked authorized, you still need reasonable third-party belief and reliance.
how_to_apply: Focus on the principal's manifestations to the third party, not the agent's own claims.
Ask whether those manifestations would make a reasonable third party believe the agent had authority.
Identify the source of that belief, such as title, prior dealings, position, communications, or conduct.
Then ask whether the third party actually and reasonably relied on that appearance of authority.
Conclude whether the principal is bound because apparent authority existed at the time of the transaction.

common_traps.title: Using the agent's statements
common_traps.explanation: The classic mistake is relying on the agent's own claim of authority. Apparent authority must come from the principal's manifestations to the third party.

common_traps.title: Skipping reliance
common_traps.explanation: Even if the agent looked authorized, you still need reasonable third-party belief and reliance.

rules.exam_tip: For apparent authority, focus on the principal's manifestations to the third party and the reasonableness of the third party's reliance. The agent's own claim of authority is not enough.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Inherent Agency Power
rules.prompt_question: What is inherent agency power?
rules.rule_statement: Inherent agency power is an older doctrine under which a principal may be bound by certain acts of an agent that are usual or incidental to the agent's position, even absent actual authority, particularly to protect third parties dealing with an agent of an undisclosed principal.
rules.application_example: A manager of a business buys ordinary goods from a supplier, binding an undisclosed owner even if the owner privately restricted that power.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Modern analysis often prefers apparent authority or estoppel, but MEE outlines still list inherent agency power.
how_to_apply: Identify the agent's position and the kind of act the agent performed.
Ask whether the act was usual or incidental to that position, especially in dealing with an undisclosed principal.
Use the facts to show whether the transaction looked ordinary from the third party's perspective.
Explain why the principal may still be bound despite private restrictions on the agent.
Conclude whether the older inherent-agency-power doctrine applies.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Modern analysis often prefers apparent authority or estoppel, but MEE outlines still list inherent agency power.

rules.exam_tip: If inherent agency power appears, treat it as an older protective doctrine. Use it carefully and explain why the act was usual or incidental to the agent's position.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Agency by Estoppel
rules.prompt_question: When is a person estopped to deny agency?
rules.rule_statement: A person may be estopped to deny agency when the person intentionally or carelessly causes a third party to believe another has authority to act for the person, and the third party justifiably and detrimentally relies on that belief.
rules.application_example: A business allows someone to appear as its purchasing agent, and a vendor extends credit in reliance on that appearance.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Estoppel focuses on fairness and detrimental reliance, not true consent between principal and agent.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the principal intentionally or carelessly created the appearance that another person had authority.
Then identify the third party's reliance and explain why that reliance was justified.
Show the detriment suffered because the third party acted in reliance on the appearance of authority.
Keep the analysis focused on fairness and reliance, not actual consent between principal and agent.
Conclude whether the principal is estopped from denying the agency relationship.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Estoppel focuses on fairness and detrimental reliance, not true consent between principal and agent.

rules.exam_tip: For estoppel, focus on fairness and reliance. Show what the principal caused the third party to believe and what detriment the third party suffered by relying on that appearance.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Ratification
rules.prompt_question: How does a principal ratify an unauthorized act?
rules.rule_statement: A principal ratifies an unauthorized act by affirming it after the fact or by accepting its benefits with knowledge of the material facts. Ratification retroactively creates the effect of authority if the principal had capacity and the act could have been authorized at the time.
rules.application_example: A principal learns an agent made an unauthorized purchase and keeps the goods, thereby ratifying the contract.
rules.common_trap: Ignoring knowledge: Ratification requires knowledge of the material facts. Acceptance of benefits without that knowledge is not enough. Forgetting timing and capacity: Students also forget that the principal must have capacity and that ratification cannot unfairly prejudice intervening third-party rights.
how_to_apply: Start with the unauthorized act and identify exactly what the agent did without authority.
Ask whether the principal later affirmed the act or accepted its benefits.
Then ask whether the principal had knowledge of the material facts and had capacity to ratify.
Check whether ratification would unfairly prejudice third-party rights that arose before ratification.
Conclude whether the act became binding retroactively through ratification.

common_traps.title: Ignoring knowledge
common_traps.explanation: Ratification requires knowledge of the material facts. Acceptance of benefits without that knowledge is not enough.

common_traps.title: Forgetting timing and capacity
common_traps.explanation: Students also forget that the principal must have capacity and that ratification cannot unfairly prejudice intervening third-party rights.

rules.exam_tip: When facts show the principal later accepted benefits, paid, performed, or stayed silent after learning the facts, check ratification. Knowledge of material facts is the key point.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Agent Liability on Contract for Disclosed Principal
rules.prompt_question: When is an agent liable on a contract made for a disclosed principal?
rules.rule_statement: An agent who enters a contract on behalf of a disclosed principal is generally not liable on the contract if the agent had authority and the contract indicates representative capacity.
rules.application_example: An agent signs 'ABC Corp., by Jane Doe, Agent' and is not personally liable if ABC is disclosed and authorized.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: An agent may still be liable if the agent lacked authority or expressly agreed to be bound.
how_to_apply: First classify the principal as disclosed at the time of contracting.
Then ask whether the agent acted with authority and whether the contract showed representative capacity.
Look closely at the signature line and contract wording to see whether the agent signed personally or as a representative.
If authority was missing or the agent agreed to be bound personally, explain why liability can still attach.
Conclude whether the agent is personally liable on the contract.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: An agent may still be liable if the agent lacked authority or expressly agreed to be bound.

rules.exam_tip: For agent contract liability, first classify the principal as disclosed, partially disclosed, or undisclosed. Then analyze authority and whether the agent clearly signed in representative capacity.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Agent Liability for Undisclosed or Partially Disclosed Principal
rules.prompt_question: When is an agent liable on a contract for an undisclosed or partially disclosed principal?
rules.rule_statement: An agent who contracts on behalf of an undisclosed or partially disclosed principal is generally liable on the contract unless the parties agree otherwise.
rules.application_example: An agent buys goods without revealing the principal and is personally liable to the seller.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The third party may often elect to hold either the principal or the agent liable once the principal is discovered.
how_to_apply: Begin by classifying the principal as undisclosed or partially disclosed.
Then explain that the third party did not receive full disclosure at the time of contracting.
Apply the rule that the agent is generally liable unless the parties agreed otherwise.
If the principal is later discovered, explain whether the third party may elect to pursue the principal, the agent, or both where allowed.
Conclude who remains liable on the contract.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The third party may often elect to hold either the principal or the agent liable once the principal is discovered.

rules.exam_tip: For agent contract liability, first classify the principal as disclosed, partially disclosed, or undisclosed. Then analyze authority and whether the agent clearly signed in representative capacity.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Principal Liability on Authorized Contracts
rules.prompt_question: When is a principal liable on a contract made by an agent?
rules.rule_statement: A principal is liable on a contract made by an agent acting with actual authority, apparent authority, inherent agency power where recognized, or ratified authority.
rules.application_example: An authorized sales agent signs a supply contract that binds the principal company.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: If no authority or ratification exists, the principal is usually not bound unless estoppel applies.
how_to_apply: Identify the theory that could bind the principal: actual authority, apparent authority, inherent agency power, estoppel, or ratification.
Apply the facts separately under the strongest theory instead of blending the doctrines together.
Show exactly how the agent's act fits one binding theory or why no theory fits.
If authority is disputed, explain who received the relevant manifestation and when.
Conclude whether the principal is bound on the contract.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: If no authority or ratification exists, the principal is usually not bound unless estoppel applies.

rules.exam_tip: When a principal may be bound on a contract, organize the answer by theory: actual authority, apparent authority, inherent authority where recognized, estoppel, or ratification.

rules.subtopic: Power of Agent to Bind Principal
rules.title: Implied Warranty of Authority
rules.prompt_question: What liability does an agent face when acting without authority?
rules.rule_statement: An agent who purports to act for a principal impliedly warrants to the third party that the agent has authority. If the agent lacks authority and the principal is not bound, the agent may be liable for breach of that implied warranty of authority.
rules.application_example: An agent signs a lease for a company without authorization and may be liable to the landlord if the company is not bound.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The remedy is not always the contract price; it is usually damages for loss caused by the false representation of authority.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the agent purported to act for a principal without actual authority.
Then determine whether the principal is not bound because the authority was missing.
Explain what representation of authority the agent effectively made to the third party.
Identify the loss caused by the third party's reliance on that false authority.
Conclude whether the agent is liable for breach of the implied warranty of authority.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The remedy is not always the contract price; it is usually damages for loss caused by the false representation of authority.

rules.exam_tip: If the principal is not bound because the agent lacked authority, check whether the agent is personally liable for falsely implying authority.

rules.title: Termination of Actual Authority
rules.prompt_question: How is actual authority terminated?
rules.rule_statement: Actual authority terminates by revocation by the principal, renunciation by the agent, occurrence of circumstances causing the agent reasonably to believe the principal no longer consents, completion of the task, lapse of time, or death or loss of capacity of the principal or agent.
rules.application_example: A principal revokes an agent's authority before the agent signs the contract.
rules.common_trap: Wrong perspective: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent. Blending authority doctrines: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.
how_to_apply: Identify the source of the agent's actual authority before the alleged termination.
Then ask what ended that authority: revocation, renunciation, completion, lapse of time, death, incapacity, or changed circumstances.
Match the timing carefully. The key question is whether authority still existed when the agent acted.
Separate actual authority from apparent authority because ending one does not automatically end the other.
Conclude whether actual authority had terminated before the transaction or tort occurred.

common_traps.title: Wrong perspective
common_traps.explanation: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent.

common_traps.title: Blending authority doctrines
common_traps.explanation: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.

rules.exam_tip: For actual authority, start with communications between the principal and agent. Do not use the third party's belief unless you are separately analyzing apparent authority.

rules.title: Termination of Apparent Authority
rules.prompt_question: How is apparent authority terminated?
rules.rule_statement: Apparent authority terminates when it is no longer reasonable for the third party to believe the agent is authorized, usually after notice from the principal or other facts that make reliance unreasonable.
rules.application_example: A principal informs customers that a former employee no longer has authority to act for the business.
rules.common_trap: Using the agent's statements: The classic mistake is relying on the agent's own claim of authority. Apparent authority must come from the principal's manifestations to the third party. Skipping reliance: Even if the agent looked authorized, you still need reasonable third-party belief and reliance.
how_to_apply: Start from the third party's perspective and ask whether continued reliance remained reasonable.
Identify what notice or changed facts reached the third party and when.
Explain whether the principal took sufficient steps to inform prior customers or counterparties.
Distinguish termination of actual authority from termination of apparent authority.
Conclude whether apparent authority still lingered or was effectively cut off.

common_traps.title: Using the agent's statements
common_traps.explanation: The classic mistake is relying on the agent's own claim of authority. Apparent authority must come from the principal's manifestations to the third party.

common_traps.title: Skipping reliance
common_traps.explanation: Even if the agent looked authorized, you still need reasonable third-party belief and reliance.

rules.exam_tip: For apparent authority, focus on the principal's manifestations to the third party and the reasonableness of the third party's reliance. The agent's own claim of authority is not enough.

rules.title: Respondeat Superior
rules.prompt_question: When is a principal or employer vicariously liable for an agent's tort?
rules.rule_statement: An employer is vicariously liable for torts committed by an employee within the scope of employment under the doctrine of respondeat superior.
rules.application_example: A delivery driver negligently causes an accident while making deliveries for the employer.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The key issue is whether the tortfeasor is an employee rather than an independent contractor and whether the conduct was within the scope of employment.
how_to_apply: First determine whether the tortfeasor was an employee rather than an independent contractor.
Then ask whether the tort occurred within the scope of employment.
Use the facts to show whether the employee was performing assigned work or furthering the employer's business at the time.
If the conduct looks personal, explain whether it was only a minor deviation or a true departure from the job.
Conclude whether the employer is vicariously liable.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The key issue is whether the tortfeasor is an employee rather than an independent contractor and whether the conduct was within the scope of employment.

rules.exam_tip: For respondeat superior, write the analysis in two steps: employee status first, scope of employment second. If the conduct looks personal, discuss frolic versus detour.

rules.title: Scope of Employment
rules.prompt_question: How do courts determine whether conduct was within the scope of employment?
rules.rule_statement: Conduct is within the scope of employment if it is of the kind the employee was employed to perform, occurs substantially within authorized time and space limits, and is motivated at least in part by a purpose to serve the employer.
rules.application_example: A cashier negligently injures a customer while stocking merchandise during a work shift.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: A substantial personal frolic may remove conduct from the scope, while a minor detour usually does not.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the conduct was the kind of work the employee was hired to perform.
Then check whether it happened substantially within authorized time and space limits.
Next ask whether the employee was at least partly motivated by a purpose to serve the employer.
Use the facts to distinguish job-related conduct from purely personal conduct.
Conclude whether the act falls inside or outside the scope of employment.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: A substantial personal frolic may remove conduct from the scope, while a minor detour usually does not.

rules.exam_tip: For respondeat superior, write the analysis in two steps: employee status first, scope of employment second. If the conduct looks personal, discuss frolic versus detour.

rules.title: Frolic and Detour
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between a frolic and a detour?
rules.rule_statement: A detour is a minor deviation from assigned work that remains within the scope of employment, while a frolic is a substantial departure for personal purposes that takes the employee outside the scope of employment until the employee returns to the employer's business.
rules.application_example: A driver briefly stops for coffee while on a delivery route, which is likely a detour rather than a frolic.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Once the employee resumes the employer's work, scope of employment may be restored.
how_to_apply: Identify the employee's assigned route or work objective before the deviation occurred.
Then measure the size and purpose of the deviation from the employer's business.
A small personal sidetrack is usually a detour, but a substantial personal mission is a frolic.
If the employee later returned to the employer's business, explain when scope of employment resumed.
Conclude whether liability attaches at the moment of the tort.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Once the employee resumes the employer's work, scope of employment may be restored.

rules.exam_tip: For respondeat superior, write the analysis in two steps: employee status first, scope of employment second. If the conduct looks personal, discuss frolic versus detour.

rules.title: Employer Liability for Intentional Torts
rules.prompt_question: When is an employer liable for an employee's intentional torts?
rules.rule_statement: An employer may be vicariously liable for an employee's intentional tort if the act was within the scope of employment, such as when force is job-related, the act was motivated by a purpose to serve the employer, or the conduct was foreseeable in the employment context.
rules.application_example: A bouncer uses excessive force while removing a patron from a club.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Purely personal assaults usually fall outside the scope unless the job makes such confrontations foreseeable.
how_to_apply: Start by identifying the intentional tort and the employee's job role.
Ask whether force or confrontation was job-related, foreseeable, or motivated in part by serving the employer.
Use the facts to distinguish work-related aggression from a purely personal dispute.
Explain why the employment context either makes the act foreseeable or takes it outside the scope of employment.
Conclude whether the employer is vicariously liable for the intentional tort.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Purely personal assaults usually fall outside the scope unless the job makes such confrontations foreseeable.

rules.exam_tip: For respondeat superior, write the analysis in two steps: employee status first, scope of employment second. If the conduct looks personal, discuss frolic versus detour.

rules.title: Independent Contractor Rule
rules.prompt_question: Is a principal generally liable for torts of an independent contractor?
rules.rule_statement: A principal is generally not vicariously liable for torts committed by an independent contractor because the principal lacks the right to control the manner and means of the contractor's work.
rules.application_example: A homeowner generally is not liable for a painter's negligence if the painter is an independent contractor.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: There are important exceptions, such as inherently dangerous activities or nondelegable duties.
how_to_apply: Begin by determining whether the tortfeasor was truly an independent contractor rather than an employee.
Focus on the principal's right to control the manner and means of the work.
If the principal lacked that control, explain why the default rule is no vicarious liability.
Then check separately whether an exception might still impose liability.
Conclude whether the principal escapes liability under the general rule.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: There are important exceptions, such as inherently dangerous activities or nondelegable duties.

rules.exam_tip: For independent-contractor issues, start with the general no-liability rule, then test recognized exceptions such as retained control, negligent selection, inherently dangerous activity, or nondelegable duty.

rules.title: Exceptions for Independent Contractors
rules.prompt_question: What are common exceptions to the general rule against liability for independent contractors?
rules.rule_statement: A principal may be liable for an independent contractor's torts when the principal is negligent in selection, when the work is inherently dangerous, when a nondelegable duty is involved, or when the principal retains substantial control over the work.
rules.application_example: A landowner may be liable for blasting operations performed by an independent contractor because blasting is inherently dangerous.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The exception is based on the nature of the activity or duty, not merely the existence of a contract.
how_to_apply: Start with the general rule that there is usually no vicarious liability for an independent contractor.
Then test the recognized exceptions one by one: negligent selection, inherently dangerous activity, nondelegable duty, or retained control.
Use the facts to explain why the activity or duty falls within a specific exception.
Do not just list exceptions. Tie one concrete exception to the actual facts.
Conclude whether the principal is liable despite the independent-contractor relationship.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The exception is based on the nature of the activity or duty, not merely the existence of a contract.

rules.exam_tip: For independent-contractor issues, start with the general no-liability rule, then test recognized exceptions such as retained control, negligent selection, inherently dangerous activity, or nondelegable duty.

rules.title: Principal's Direct Liability
rules.prompt_question: When is a principal directly liable for an agent's conduct?
rules.rule_statement: A principal may be directly liable for its own negligence in selecting, supervising, or retaining an agent, or for authorizing or ratifying wrongful conduct.
rules.application_example: An employer hires a driver with a known history of reckless driving and may be directly liable for negligent hiring.
rules.common_trap: Ignoring knowledge: Ratification requires knowledge of the material facts. Acceptance of benefits without that knowledge is not enough. Forgetting timing and capacity: Students also forget that the principal must have capacity and that ratification cannot unfairly prejudice intervening third-party rights.
how_to_apply: Separate direct liability from vicarious liability at the start of your analysis.
Ask whether the principal itself acted negligently in hiring, supervising, retaining, authorizing, or ratifying wrongful conduct.
Point to facts showing what the principal knew or should have known.
Explain how that negligence or ratification caused the plaintiff's injury.
Conclude whether the principal is directly liable for its own conduct.

common_traps.title: Ignoring knowledge
common_traps.explanation: Ratification requires knowledge of the material facts. Acceptance of benefits without that knowledge is not enough.

common_traps.title: Forgetting timing and capacity
common_traps.explanation: Students also forget that the principal must have capacity and that ratification cannot unfairly prejudice intervening third-party rights.

rules.exam_tip: Separate direct liability from vicarious liability. Direct liability focuses on the principal's own negligence in hiring, supervision, retention, authorization, or ratification.

rules.title: Agent's Duty of Loyalty
rules.prompt_question: What is an agent's duty of loyalty?
rules.rule_statement: An agent owes the principal a duty of loyalty and must act solely for the principal's benefit in matters connected with the agency, avoiding self-dealing, usurpation of business opportunities, undisclosed conflicts of interest, and secret profits.
rules.application_example: An agent cannot secretly accept a kickback from a supplier while purchasing for the principal.
rules.common_trap: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company. Forgetting cleansing mechanisms: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.
how_to_apply: Identify the agency relationship and the transaction or conduct being challenged.
Ask whether the agent acted for personal benefit instead of solely for the principal's benefit in matters connected to the agency.
Look for self-dealing, secret profits, conflicts of interest, kickbacks, competition, or usurped opportunities.
Then ask whether the principal gave informed consent after full disclosure.
Conclude whether the agent breached the duty of loyalty and what remedy may follow.

common_traps.title: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong
common_traps.explanation: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company.

common_traps.title: Forgetting cleansing mechanisms
common_traps.explanation: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.

rules.exam_tip: For agency fiduciary-duty issues, identify the specific duty first. Loyalty focuses on conflicts and secret benefits; care focuses on competence; obedience focuses on lawful instructions.

rules.title: Agent's Duty of Care
rules.prompt_question: What duty of care does an agent owe?
rules.rule_statement: An agent owes the principal a duty to act with the care, competence, and diligence normally exercised by agents in similar circumstances.
rules.application_example: A real-estate agent must use reasonable diligence in presenting offers to the principal.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The standard may be heightened if the agent claims special skills or expertise.
how_to_apply: Identify the task the agent undertook for the principal.
Ask what level of care, competence, and diligence a similar agent would use in those circumstances.
Compare the agent's conduct to that standard using the facts, not vague labels.
If the agent claimed special expertise, explain why the expected standard may be higher.
Conclude whether the agent breached the duty of care.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The standard may be heightened if the agent claims special skills or expertise.

rules.title: Agent's Duty of Obedience
rules.prompt_question: What is the agent's duty of obedience?
rules.rule_statement: An agent must act within the scope of actual authority and obey the principal's lawful instructions concerning the agency relationship.
rules.application_example: An agent instructed to buy no more than 500 units breaches the duty of obedience by ordering 1,000 units.
rules.common_trap: Wrong perspective: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent. Blending authority doctrines: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.
how_to_apply: Start with the principal's lawful instructions and the scope of actual authority.
Then compare the agent's conduct to those instructions.
Explain precisely where the agent followed, exceeded, or ignored the principal's directions.
Check whether the instruction was lawful because an agent need not obey unlawful commands.
Conclude whether the duty of obedience was breached.

common_traps.title: Wrong perspective
common_traps.explanation: Students often analyze the third party's belief first. Actual authority is about what the principal communicated to the agent.

common_traps.title: Blending authority doctrines
common_traps.explanation: Do not mix actual authority with apparent authority. Keep principal-to-agent communications separate from principal-to-third-party manifestations.

rules.title: Principal's Duties to Agent
rules.prompt_question: What duties does a principal owe an agent?
rules.rule_statement: A principal generally owes the agent duties of compensation as agreed, reimbursement and indemnification for authorized expenses and liabilities, and cooperation in the agency relationship.
rules.application_example: A principal reimburses an agent for reasonable travel expenses incurred while carrying out authorized business.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: An agent usually is not entitled to reimbursement for expenses resulting from the agent's own fault or unauthorized conduct.
how_to_apply: Identify the expense, liability, compensation, or cooperation problem between principal and agent.
Ask whether the agent acted within authority and in the ordinary course of the agency relationship.
If the agent seeks reimbursement or indemnification, connect the expense to authorized conduct.
If the expense arose from the agent's own wrongful or unauthorized conduct, explain why recovery may be limited.
Conclude what duty the principal owed and whether it was breached.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: An agent usually is not entitled to reimbursement for expenses resulting from the agent's own fault or unauthorized conduct.

rules.title: General Partnership Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is a general partnership formed?
rules.rule_statement: A general partnership is formed when two or more persons associate to carry on as co-owners a business for profit, whether or not they intend to form a partnership.
rules.application_example: Two friends open a catering business and share profits without filing any documents, creating a general partnership.
rules.common_trap: Trusting labels: Calling someone a lender, consultant, or friend does not decide the issue. Courts look to substance, especially co-ownership and profit sharing. Forgetting exceptions to profit sharing: Profit sharing creates a presumption, but wages, debt repayment, rent, and loan interest may explain the payment without creating a partnership.
how_to_apply: Ask whether two or more persons were carrying on as co-owners a business for profit.
Use objective facts such as profit sharing, control, contributions, and business conduct rather than labels alone.
If profit sharing is present, analyze whether an exception explains it, such as wages, rent, debt repayment, or loan interest.
Do not treat lack of formal filing or lack of intent to form a partnership as controlling.
Conclude whether a general partnership arose by operation of law.

rules.title: Profit Sharing Presumption
rules.prompt_question: When does profit sharing create a presumption of partnership?
rules.rule_statement: A person who receives a share of partnership profits is presumed to be a partner unless the profits were received in payment of a debt, wages, rent, interest on a loan, or for certain other exceptions.
rules.application_example: A person who receives 30 percent of net profits from a business is presumed to be a partner absent an exception.

rules.title: No Filing Requirement for General Partnership
rules.prompt_question: Must a general partnership file a certificate to be formed?
rules.rule_statement: A general partnership need not file any organizational document to exist; it can arise by operation of law from the parties' conduct.
rules.application_example: A consulting business run jointly by two people can be a partnership even though no partnership papers were filed.

rules.title: Partner Authority
rules.prompt_question: When can a partner bind the partnership?
rules.rule_statement: Each partner is an agent of the partnership for carrying on the partnership's business in the ordinary course, and an act of a partner for apparently carrying on the ordinary course of the partnership business binds the partnership unless the partner lacked authority and the third party knew or had notice of that lack of authority.
rules.application_example: A partner in a law firm hires ordinary office services, binding the partnership.
rules.common_trap: Misclassifying the act: Students often assume a partner can always bind the partnership. The key question is whether the act was in the ordinary course of the partnership business. Ignoring the third party's knowledge: If the third party knew the partner lacked authority, the partnership may not be bound even for an otherwise ordinary act.
how_to_apply: Identify the act taken by the partner and the nature of the partnership's business.
Ask whether the act was apparently for carrying on the partnership business in the ordinary course.
If it was ordinary, explain why the partnership is bound unless the third party knew of a lack of authority.
If it was not ordinary, move to the approval analysis instead of assuming authority.
Conclude whether the partner's act bound the partnership.

common_traps.title: Misclassifying the act
common_traps.explanation: Students often assume a partner can always bind the partnership. The key question is whether the act was in the ordinary course of the partnership business.

common_traps.title: Ignoring the third party's knowledge
common_traps.explanation: If the third party knew the partner lacked authority, the partnership may not be bound even for an otherwise ordinary act.

rules.exam_tip: For partner authority, compare the act to the partnership's actual business. Ordinary-course acts may bind the partnership; extraordinary acts usually require authorization.

rules.title: Acts Outside the Ordinary Course
rules.prompt_question: How are partner acts outside the ordinary course treated?
rules.rule_statement: An act of a partner that is not apparently for carrying on the partnership business in the ordinary course binds the partnership only if the act was authorized by the other partners.
rules.application_example: A partner selling substantially all partnership assets without approval usually does not bind the partnership.
rules.common_trap: Misclassifying the act: Students often assume a partner can always bind the partnership. The key question is whether the act was in the ordinary course of the partnership business. Ignoring the third party's knowledge: If the third party knew the partner lacked authority, the partnership may not be bound even for an otherwise ordinary act.
how_to_apply: Identify the challenged act and explain why it falls outside the ordinary course of the partnership business.
Because the act is extraordinary, ask whether the other partners actually authorized it.
Do not assume ordinary-course agency power applies to major asset sales or structural acts.
Use the facts to show whether the required partner approval was or was not obtained.
Conclude whether the partnership is bound.

rules.title: Partner Liability for Partnership Obligations
rules.prompt_question: What is a partner's liability for partnership obligations?
rules.rule_statement: In a general partnership, all partners are jointly and severally liable for all obligations of the partnership unless otherwise provided by law.
rules.application_example: A creditor of the partnership can sue one partner for the full amount of a partnership debt.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: A partner who pays more than that partner's share may seek contribution from the other partners.
how_to_apply: First determine whether the obligation is a partnership obligation.
If it is, explain that general partners are jointly and severally liable.
Then distinguish external liability to the creditor from internal contribution rights among partners.
Use the facts to show whether the claimant may pursue one partner, several partners, or the partnership itself.
Conclude what liability each partner faces.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: A partner who pays more than that partner's share may seek contribution from the other partners.

rules.exam_tip: Separate partnership liability from partner personal liability. A partnership may be liable, and general partners may also face personal liability for partnership obligations.

rules.title: Partnership Liability for Partner Torts
rules.prompt_question: When is the partnership liable for a partner's tort?
rules.rule_statement: A partnership is liable for loss or injury caused to a person by a partner's wrongful act or omission acting in the ordinary course of the partnership business or with authority of the partnership.
rules.application_example: A law firm may be liable for a partner's malpractice committed while representing a client.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The partnership's liability does not eliminate the individual tortfeasor's own liability.
how_to_apply: Identify the wrongful act and the partner who committed it.
Ask whether the act occurred in the ordinary course of partnership business or with partnership authority.
If yes, explain why the partnership is liable in addition to the individual wrongdoer.
Then address whether the partners also face personal liability under general partnership rules.
Conclude who is liable to the injured party.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The partnership's liability does not eliminate the individual tortfeasor's own liability.

rules.exam_tip: Separate partnership liability from partner personal liability. A partnership may be liable, and general partners may also face personal liability for partnership obligations.

rules.title: Partner Duty of Loyalty
rules.prompt_question: What is a partner's duty of loyalty?
rules.rule_statement: A partner owes the partnership and the other partners a duty of loyalty, which includes accounting for benefits derived from partnership business or use of partnership property, refraining from dealing with the partnership on behalf of an adverse interest, and refraining from competing with the partnership before dissolution.
rules.application_example: A partner cannot secretly divert a partnership client to the partner's own separate business.
rules.common_trap: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company. Forgetting cleansing mechanisms: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.
how_to_apply: Identify the partnership opportunity, benefit, adverse dealing, or competition at issue.
Ask whether the partner personally benefited from partnership business or property without proper disclosure.
Look for diversion of clients, secret profits, competing ventures, or transactions on behalf of an adverse interest.
If there was consent or agreement modification, explain whether it is effective under the statute.
Conclude whether the partner breached the duty of loyalty.

common_traps.title: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong
common_traps.explanation: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company.

common_traps.title: Forgetting cleansing mechanisms
common_traps.explanation: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.

rules.exam_tip: For partner fiduciary duties, do not automatically use corporate standards. Partnership duty of care often uses a heightened threshold, while loyalty focuses on secret profits, competition, and adverse dealing.

rules.title: Partner Duty of Care
rules.prompt_question: What duty of care does a partner owe?
rules.rule_statement: A partner owes the partnership and the other partners a duty of care limited to refraining from grossly negligent or reckless conduct, intentional misconduct, or a knowing violation of law.
rules.application_example: A partner who knowingly violates environmental law while acting for the partnership breaches the duty of care.
rules.common_trap: Using ordinary negligence: Partnership duty-of-care questions often use the lower statutory threshold of gross negligence, recklessness, intentional misconduct, or knowing violation of law, not simple negligence. Forgetting the statute: Students lose points by applying corporate-style fiduciary standards without checking the partnership statute.
how_to_apply: Begin with the statutory partnership standard, which is usually gross negligence, reckless conduct, intentional misconduct, or knowing violation of law.
Do not automatically use ordinary negligence as the measure.
Match the partner's conduct to that heightened threshold using specific facts.
Explain why the conduct either crosses or falls short of that threshold.
Conclude whether the duty of care was breached.

common_traps.title: Using ordinary negligence
common_traps.explanation: Partnership duty-of-care questions often use the lower statutory threshold of gross negligence, recklessness, intentional misconduct, or knowing violation of law, not simple negligence.

common_traps.explanation: Students lose points by applying corporate-style fiduciary standards without checking the partnership statute.

rules.title: Management Rights
rules.prompt_question: How are management rights allocated in a general partnership?
rules.rule_statement: Absent agreement otherwise, all partners have equal rights in the management and conduct of the partnership business, and ordinary matters are decided by a majority of the partners while matters outside the ordinary course generally require unanimous consent.
rules.application_example: Two of three partners can decide an ordinary vendor contract, but admitting a new partner requires unanimity unless agreed otherwise.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Voting rights are usually equal per partner, not proportional to capital contribution, absent agreement.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the dispute concerns ordinary management or a matter outside the ordinary course.
Apply the default rule that partners have equal management rights absent agreement otherwise.
Use the facts to determine whether majority approval is enough or unanimity is required.
Do not confuse management voting rules with authority to bind third parties.
Conclude whether the action was validly approved inside the partnership.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Voting rights are usually equal per partner, not proportional to capital contribution, absent agreement.

rules.exam_tip: For internal partnership rights, start with the partnership agreement. If there is no agreement, apply statutory defaults carefully and separate economic rights from management rights.

rules.title: Profits and Losses
rules.prompt_question: How are profits and losses shared absent agreement?
rules.rule_statement: Absent agreement otherwise, partners share profits equally and share losses in the same proportion as profits.
rules.application_example: If two partners have no agreement, they split both profits and losses 50-50 even if one contributed more capital.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Students often assume contributions determine shares, but the default rule is equal sharing.
how_to_apply: Start with the partnership agreement, if any, because it can alter the default allocation rules.
If there is no agreement, apply the default rule that profits are shared equally and losses follow profits.
Do not assume capital contribution amounts control unless the agreement says so.
Use the facts to separate internal allocation from outside creditor liability.
Conclude how profits and losses are shared.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Students often assume contributions determine shares, but the default rule is equal sharing.

rules.exam_tip: For internal partnership rights, start with the partnership agreement. If there is no agreement, apply statutory defaults carefully and separate economic rights from management rights.

rules.title: Indemnification and Reimbursement
rules.prompt_question: When is a partnership required to indemnify a partner?
rules.rule_statement: A partnership must indemnify a partner for liabilities incurred in the ordinary course of the partnership's business or for preservation of its business or property, and reimburse a partner for payments made and liabilities incurred in the ordinary course or for preservation of the business or property.
rules.application_example: A partner who pays a partnership debt out of personal funds is entitled to reimbursement from the partnership.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The right may be limited when the liability resulted from the partner's wrongful conduct.
how_to_apply: Identify the payment, liability, or expense the partner is seeking to shift to the partnership.
Ask whether it was incurred in the ordinary course of partnership business or to preserve partnership property.
If the expense arose from unauthorized or wrongful conduct, explain why reimbursement may be denied.
Separate reimbursement to the paying partner from contribution among partners.
Conclude whether the partnership must indemnify or reimburse.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The right may be limited when the liability resulted from the partner's wrongful conduct.

rules.exam_tip: For internal partnership rights, start with the partnership agreement. If there is no agreement, apply statutory defaults carefully and separate economic rights from management rights.

rules.title: Transfer of Partnership Interest
rules.prompt_question: What may a partner transfer without dissolving the partnership?
rules.rule_statement: A partner may transfer the partner's transferable interest, which generally consists of the partner's share of profits, losses, and distributions, but a transferee does not become a partner or acquire management rights without consent of the other partners.
rules.application_example: A partner assigns the right to receive future distributions to a creditor, but the creditor does not become a voting partner.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Economic rights are transferable; governance rights are not automatically transferable.
how_to_apply: Identify exactly what the partner transferred: economic rights or full partner status.
Apply the rule that a transferee usually gets only the transferable economic interest, not management rights.
Ask whether the other partners consented to admission as a full partner.
Do not confuse the right to receive distributions with the right to vote or manage.
Conclude what rights the transferee actually acquired.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Economic rights are transferable; governance rights are not automatically transferable.

rules.exam_tip: For internal partnership rights, start with the partnership agreement. If there is no agreement, apply statutory defaults carefully and separate economic rights from management rights.

rules.title: Partnership Property
rules.prompt_question: Who owns partnership property?
rules.rule_statement: Property acquired by a partnership is property of the partnership and not of the partners individually.
rules.application_example: Real estate purchased in the partnership's name belongs to the partnership, not directly to the individual partners.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: A partner cannot use partnership property for purely personal purposes absent authorization.
how_to_apply: Identify the property at issue and ask whether it was acquired by or for the partnership.
If it is partnership property, explain that it belongs to the partnership entity, not the partners individually.
Use title, source of funds, and business purpose facts to classify the property.
Do not treat a partner's ownership interest as direct ownership of specific partnership assets.
Conclude who owns and may control the property.

rules.title: Dissociation
rules.prompt_question: What is dissociation?
rules.rule_statement: Dissociation is the change in the relationship among the partners caused by a partner's ceasing to be associated in carrying on the business. It may be rightful or wrongful and does not always cause dissolution.
rules.application_example: A partner withdraws from a continuing partnership, causing dissociation but not necessarily winding up.
rules.common_trap: Skipping approval or trigger analysis: Students often jump to the result without first identifying what event occurred and what approval, filing, or statutory trigger was actually required. Blending distinct structural steps: Keep separate issues separate. Do not blend dissociation with dissolution, or board approval with shareholder approval, or winding up with ongoing business operations.
how_to_apply: Start by distinguishing dissociation from dissolution because they are not the same issue.
Identify the event that caused the partner to cease being associated with the business.
Ask whether the partnership continues or whether the event also triggered winding up.
Then identify the legal consequences for the departing partner and the continuing partnership.
Conclude the partnership's status after the dissociation.

rules.title: Wrongful Dissociation
rules.prompt_question: When is a partner's dissociation wrongful?
rules.rule_statement: A partner's dissociation is wrongful if it breaches an express term of the partnership agreement or occurs before expiration of a term or completion of an undertaking in certain ways, such as wrongful withdrawal or bankruptcy where treated as wrongful by statute or agreement.
rules.application_example: A partner leaves a partnership formed for a fixed five-year term after one year without justification.

rules.title: Dissolution and Winding Up
rules.prompt_question: What is dissolution, and what follows it?
rules.rule_statement: Dissolution is the event triggering the winding up of partnership business. After dissolution, the partnership continues only for the purpose of winding up, including completing unfinished business, collecting assets, paying creditors, and distributing the surplus.

rules.prompt_question: What events generally cause dissolution of a general partnership?
rules.rule_statement: Dissolution may occur by the partners' will, expiration of a term or completion of an undertaking, an event agreed upon in the partnership agreement, judicial decree, or in some cases certain partner dissociations in an at-will partnership.

rules.title: Liability After Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: Can a partnership be bound after dissolution?
rules.rule_statement: After dissolution, a partnership may be bound by acts appropriate for winding up or by acts that would have bound the partnership before dissolution if the third party lacked notice of the dissolution, subject to statutory filing and notice provisions.

rules.title: Limited Liability Partnership
rules.prompt_question: How is a limited liability partnership created and what is its key effect?
rules.rule_statement: A limited liability partnership is created by filing the required statement with the state, and its principal effect is that partners are generally not personally liable for obligations of the partnership solely by reason of being partners.
rules.application_example: A law firm registers as an LLP so that partners are generally shielded from personal liability for firm debts and malpractice of other partners.
