rules.title: Limited Partnership Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is a limited partnership formed?
rules.rule_statement: A limited partnership is formed by filing a certificate of limited partnership or similar document with the appropriate state office, naming at least one general partner and one or more limited partners.
rules.application_example: Investors form a limited partnership for a real-estate fund by filing the required certificate.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Unlike a general partnership, a limited partnership does not arise merely from conduct without statutory filing.
how_to_apply: Start with statutory filing because a limited partnership does not arise by conduct alone.
Ask whether the required certificate or equivalent filing was made.
Then identify the required structure, including at least one general partner and limited partner.
Do not treat general-partnership default rules as enough to create an LP.
Conclude whether a valid limited partnership was formed.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Unlike a general partnership, a limited partnership does not arise merely from conduct without statutory filing.

rules.exam_tip: For limited partnership questions, classify the actor first. General partners, limited partners, and the LP itself have different management, liability, and economic-rights rules.

rules.title: General Partner Liability in LP
rules.prompt_question: What is the liability of a general partner in a limited partnership?
rules.rule_statement: A general partner in a limited partnership generally has management rights and is personally liable for the obligations of the limited partnership, much like a partner in a general partnership, unless the general partner is itself an entity providing liability protection.
rules.application_example: The general partner of a limited partnership may be personally liable for an LP contract debt.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Many modern LPs use a corporation or LLC as the general partner to avoid personal liability.
how_to_apply: Identify the actor as a general partner in an LP, not a limited partner.
Apply the rule that general partners usually manage the LP and are personally liable for LP obligations.
Then ask whether the general partner is itself an entity that provides liability protection.
Separate entity-level liability from any contract or tort liability analysis.
Conclude whether personal liability attaches.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Many modern LPs use a corporation or LLC as the general partner to avoid personal liability.

rules.exam_tip: For limited partnership questions, classify the actor first. General partners, limited partners, and the LP itself have different management, liability, and economic-rights rules.

rules.title: Limited Partner Liability
rules.prompt_question: When is a limited partner personally liable?
rules.rule_statement: A limited partner is generally not personally liable for the obligations of the limited partnership solely by reason of being a limited partner, even if the limited partner participates in management under modern statutes.
rules.application_example: A limited partner votes on major structural decisions but still retains limited liability under modern law.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Older cases emphasized a control limitation, but modern statutes generally eliminate personal liability solely for participating in control.
how_to_apply: Identify the actor as a limited partner and start with the rule of limited personal liability.
Then ask whether the problem expects modern law, under which management participation alone usually does not destroy limited liability.
If older law is implicated, analyze whether the conduct created control-based liability and misleading reliance.
Separate management participation from personal guarantees or personal wrongful acts.
Conclude whether the limited partner is personally liable.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Older cases emphasized a control limitation, but modern statutes generally eliminate personal liability solely for participating in control.

rules.exam_tip: For limited partnership questions, classify the actor first. General partners, limited partners, and the LP itself have different management, liability, and economic-rights rules.

rules.title: Control Limitation
rules.prompt_question: What is the traditional control limitation for limited partners?
rules.rule_statement: Traditionally, a limited partner risked personal liability if the limited partner participated substantially in control of the business and a third party reasonably believed the limited partner was a general partner. Modern statutes usually abolish or greatly narrow this control-based liability rule.
rules.application_example: Under older law, a limited partner who actively managed operations could risk liability to third parties.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Always check whether the exam expects modern statutory treatment or recognizes the historical doctrine as part of the outline.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the problem is using the traditional control-based doctrine or modern statutory treatment.
Under the traditional view, identify whether the limited partner substantially participated in control.
Then ask whether a third party reasonably believed the limited partner was acting like a general partner.
Under modern statutes, explain that control participation alone usually does not create personal liability.
Conclude which rule governs and whether liability follows.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Always check whether the exam expects modern statutory treatment or recognizes the historical doctrine as part of the outline.

rules.exam_tip: For limited partnership questions, classify the actor first. General partners, limited partners, and the LP itself have different management, liability, and economic-rights rules.

rules.title: Economic Rights of Limited Partners
rules.prompt_question: What economic rights do limited partners typically have?
rules.rule_statement: Limited partners generally have the right to share in profits, losses, and distributions as provided by the partnership agreement, and they may transfer economic interests subject to agreement and statute.
rules.application_example: A limited partner is entitled to periodic distributions according to the LP agreement.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Economic rights do not necessarily include management or information rights beyond those granted by statute or agreement.
how_to_apply: Identify the economic right being claimed, such as profits, losses, distributions, or transfer rights.
Start with the partnership agreement because it usually controls the allocation.
Separate economic rights from management rights, which limited partners usually do not hold by default.
Use the facts to determine whether the claimant has only distribution rights or something more.
Conclude what economic entitlement exists.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Economic rights do not necessarily include management or information rights beyond those granted by statute or agreement.

rules.exam_tip: For limited partnership questions, classify the actor first. General partners, limited partners, and the LP itself have different management, liability, and economic-rights rules.

rules.title: Disclosure Requirements
rules.prompt_question: Why are disclosure requirements important for limited partnerships?
rules.rule_statement: A limited partnership must generally comply with statutory naming and filing requirements so that third parties can determine the entity's existence and identify persons with management authority.
rules.application_example: The public filing identifies the LP and its registered agent, helping third parties verify the entity.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Failure to comply may impair entity status or create problems with authority and notice.
how_to_apply: Start with the statutory filing and naming requirements for the LP.
Explain why public disclosure matters for third-party notice and entity recognition.
Ask whether the LP properly complied with the required filings and public-record obligations.
Then connect any failure to possible authority, notice, or entity-status problems.
Conclude what legal effect the disclosure failure or compliance has.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Failure to comply may impair entity status or create problems with authority and notice.

rules.exam_tip: For limited partnership questions, classify the actor first. General partners, limited partners, and the LP itself have different management, liability, and economic-rights rules.

rules.title: Corporate Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is a corporation formed?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation is formed by filing articles of incorporation with the state. Upon filing or upon the effective date stated in the articles, the corporation comes into existence as a separate legal entity.
rules.application_example: Entrepreneurs file articles with the secretary of state to create a corporation.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Promoter activity before formation is governed by pre-incorporation rules, not ordinary corporate rules.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the issue is valid formation or some later governance dispute.
For valid formation, ask whether articles of incorporation were properly filed and the corporation came into existence as a separate legal entity.
Do not analyze promoter or preincorporation liability as if the corporation already existed.
Use the filing facts and any effective-date facts carefully.
Conclude whether the corporation existed at the relevant time.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Promoter activity before formation is governed by pre-incorporation rules, not ordinary corporate rules.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate formation issues, start with filing and entity existence. If formation is defective or timing is unclear, separately analyze de facto corporation, corporation by estoppel, or promoter rules.

rules.title: Articles of Incorporation
rules.prompt_question: What information is typically required in articles of incorporation?
rules.rule_statement: Articles of incorporation generally must include the corporate name, the number of authorized shares, the street address of the corporation's initial registered office and registered agent, and the name and address of each incorporator, along with any optional provisions.
rules.application_example: The articles list the corporation's name, registered agent, and share structure.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Specific state statutes may require additional or slightly different information.
how_to_apply: Identify the filing document and ask whether it included the required statutory contents.
Use the facts to test items such as corporate name, authorized shares, registered agent, and incorporator information.
Distinguish charter provisions in the articles from internal governance rules in bylaws.
If a missing or defective term matters, explain whether the formation is defective or merely incomplete under the statute.
Conclude whether the articles satisfy the legal requirements.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Specific state statutes may require additional or slightly different information.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate formation issues, start with filing and entity existence. If formation is defective or timing is unclear, separately analyze de facto corporation, corporation by estoppel, or promoter rules.

rules.title: Bylaws
rules.prompt_question: What are bylaws?
rules.rule_statement: Bylaws are internal rules for managing the corporation and regulating its affairs. They are typically adopted by the incorporators or directors and may later be amended by shareholders or directors as allowed by statute and the articles.
rules.application_example: Bylaws may specify meeting procedures and officer positions.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Bylaws do not have to be filed with the state and cannot conflict with the articles or governing statute.
how_to_apply: Identify the bylaw provision and ask whether it governs an internal management issue.
Then test whether the bylaw conflicts with the articles of incorporation or governing statute.
Do not treat bylaws as filed charter documents.
Use the facts to determine whether the relevant body had power to adopt or amend the bylaw.
Conclude whether the bylaw provision is valid and effective.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Bylaws do not have to be filed with the state and cannot conflict with the articles or governing statute.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate formation issues, start with filing and entity existence. If formation is defective or timing is unclear, separately analyze de facto corporation, corporation by estoppel, or promoter rules.

rules.title: De Facto Corporation
rules.prompt_question: What is a de facto corporation?
rules.rule_statement: A de facto corporation may exist when there is a valid incorporation statute, a good-faith colorable attempt to comply with it, and actual use of the corporate form, protecting participants from personal liability in some jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: Founders mistakenly file defective incorporation papers but begin doing business as a corporation.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Many modern statutes limit or abolish de facto corporation doctrine, so exam treatment may depend on the jurisdiction or outline expectations.
how_to_apply: Start by asking whether the jurisdiction still recognizes de facto corporation doctrine.
If it does, test for a valid incorporation statute, a good-faith colorable attempt to comply, and actual use of the corporate form.
Use the facts to show why organizers reasonably believed the corporation existed.
Then connect that doctrine to whether personal liability should be avoided.
Conclude whether de facto corporation status applies.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Many modern statutes limit or abolish de facto corporation doctrine, so exam treatment may depend on the jurisdiction or outline expectations.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate formation issues, start with filing and entity existence. If formation is defective or timing is unclear, separately analyze de facto corporation, corporation by estoppel, or promoter rules.

rules.title: Corporation by Estoppel
rules.prompt_question: What is corporation by estoppel?
rules.rule_statement: Under corporation by estoppel, a person who treated an enterprise as a corporation in dealing with it may be estopped from later denying its corporate existence to impose personal liability on the organizers.
rules.application_example: A vendor contracts with 'XYZ, Inc.' and may be estopped from suing the shareholders personally by denying the corporation existed.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: This doctrine is equitable and fact-specific, and many modern statutes reduce its practical importance.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the claimant dealt with the enterprise as if it were a corporation.
If so, explain why the claimant may be estopped from later denying corporate existence.
Keep the analysis equitable and fact-specific rather than purely technical.
Do not confuse corporation by estoppel with de facto corporation doctrine.
Conclude whether estoppel prevents personal-liability arguments.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: This doctrine is equitable and fact-specific, and many modern statutes reduce its practical importance.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate formation issues, start with filing and entity existence. If formation is defective or timing is unclear, separately analyze de facto corporation, corporation by estoppel, or promoter rules.

rules.title: Promoter Liability on Preincorporation Contracts
rules.prompt_question: Is a promoter liable on a preincorporation contract?
rules.rule_statement: A promoter who enters a contract on behalf of a not-yet-formed corporation is personally liable on the contract unless the contract clearly provides otherwise or there is a later novation releasing the promoter.
rules.application_example: A promoter signs a lease for future corporate office space before the corporation is formed and remains liable absent novation.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Corporate adoption of the contract alone does not automatically release the promoter.
how_to_apply: Identify the preincorporation contract and ask whether the corporation existed when it was made.
Because it did not yet exist, analyze promoter liability first.
Then ask whether the contract itself limited promoter liability or whether a later novation released the promoter.
Do not assume later corporate adoption automatically releases the promoter.
Conclude whether the promoter remains personally liable.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Corporate adoption of the contract alone does not automatically release the promoter.

rules.exam_tip: For preincorporation issues, separate promoter liability from corporate adoption. Adoption may bind the corporation, but it does not automatically release the promoter.

rules.title: Corporate Adoption of Preincorporation Contracts
rules.prompt_question: How may a corporation become bound on a preincorporation contract?
rules.rule_statement: After formation, a corporation may adopt a preincorporation contract expressly or impliedly, making the corporation liable on the contract, though the promoter remains liable unless there is a novation.
rules.application_example: A newly formed corporation begins operating from leased premises and pays rent, implying adoption of the promoter's lease contract.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Adoption binds the corporation but does not itself discharge the promoter.
how_to_apply: Identify the preincorporation obligation and ask whether the later corporation expressly or impliedly adopted it.
Use conduct such as acceptance of benefits or performance to test implied adoption.
Explain that adoption can bind the corporation even though the promoter may still remain liable.
Then ask separately whether a novation released the promoter.
Conclude who is bound after adoption.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Adoption binds the corporation but does not itself discharge the promoter.

rules.exam_tip: For preincorporation issues, separate promoter liability from corporate adoption. Adoption may bind the corporation, but it does not automatically release the promoter.

rules.title: Promoter Fiduciary Duties
rules.prompt_question: What fiduciary duties does a promoter owe?
rules.rule_statement: A promoter owes fiduciary duties of good faith and fair dealing to the corporation and its investors, including the duty to disclose material facts and avoid secret profits from transactions involving the corporation.
rules.application_example: A promoter selling property to the corporation at a markup must disclose the profit to avoid liability.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The extent of disclosure required may depend on whether disclosure is made to all subscribers, directors, or an independent board.
how_to_apply: Identify the promoter transaction and the alleged undisclosed profit or conflict.
Ask whether the promoter fully disclosed all material facts to the proper decision-makers or investors.
Use the facts to analyze fairness and secret-profit concerns.
Do not collapse promoter fiduciary-duty analysis into ordinary corporate fiduciary-duty analysis after formation.
Conclude whether the promoter breached fiduciary duties.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The extent of disclosure required may depend on whether disclosure is made to all subscribers, directors, or an independent board.

rules.exam_tip: For preincorporation issues, separate promoter liability from corporate adoption. Adoption may bind the corporation, but it does not automatically release the promoter.

rules.title: Subscriptions for Shares
rules.prompt_question: What is a subscription for shares before incorporation?
rules.rule_statement: A preincorporation subscription is an offer to buy stock in the future corporation and is often irrevocable for the period specified by statute unless all subscribers agree to revoke.
rules.application_example: An investor signs a subscription agreement to purchase 1,000 shares once the corporation is formed.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Modern statutes often make preincorporation subscriptions irrevocable for a limited period, commonly six months, unless otherwise stated.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the subscription was made before incorporation.
Then ask whether the governing statute makes the subscription irrevocable for a specified period.
Use the facts to determine whether revocation was effective or whether the subscription remained binding.
Separate subscription enforceability from later share issuance mechanics.
Conclude whether the subscriber is bound.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Modern statutes often make preincorporation subscriptions irrevocable for a limited period, commonly six months, unless otherwise stated.

rules.exam_tip: For preincorporation issues, separate promoter liability from corporate adoption. Adoption may bind the corporation, but it does not automatically release the promoter.

rules.title: Piercing the Corporate Veil
rules.prompt_question: When may a court pierce the corporate veil?
rules.rule_statement: A court may pierce the corporate veil and hold shareholders personally liable when the corporation is used to perpetrate fraud, is inadequately capitalized, or is treated as the shareholders' alter ego, and failing to pierce would sanction unfairness or injustice.
rules.application_example: A shareholder strips a corporation of assets and uses it as a shell to avoid creditors.
rules.common_trap: Treating one factor as enough: No single veil-piercing factor is automatically decisive. Courts usually look at the total relationship between owner and entity. Skipping injustice analysis: Undercapitalization or commingling alone is not always enough. You still need a strong unfairness or misuse rationale.
how_to_apply: Start from the presumption that the corporation is a separate legal entity.
Then identify facts showing misuse of the entity, such as undercapitalization, commingling, failure to observe formalities, or fraud.
Ask whether respecting the corporate form would sanction unfairness or injustice.
If reverse piercing is raised, explain why courts are especially cautious because innocent creditors or shareholders may be harmed.
Conclude whether veil piercing should or should not occur on these facts.

common_traps.title: Treating one factor as enough
common_traps.explanation: No single veil-piercing factor is automatically decisive. Courts usually look at the total relationship between owner and entity.

common_traps.title: Skipping injustice analysis
common_traps.explanation: Undercapitalization or commingling alone is not always enough. You still need a strong unfairness or misuse rationale.

rules.exam_tip: For veil-piercing essays, do not list factors mechanically. Explain how the facts show misuse of the entity and why respecting limited liability would be unfair.

rules.title: Common Veil-Piercing Factors
rules.prompt_question: What factors do courts commonly consider in veil-piercing analysis?
rules.rule_statement: Common veil-piercing factors include undercapitalization, failure to observe corporate formalities, commingling of assets, treating corporate assets as personal assets, overlapping ownership and control, and using the corporation to promote fraud or injustice.
rules.application_example: A sole shareholder pays personal bills from the corporate account and never holds meetings or keeps records.
rules.common_trap: Treating one factor as enough: No single veil-piercing factor is automatically decisive. Courts usually look at the total relationship between owner and entity. Skipping injustice analysis: Undercapitalization or commingling alone is not always enough. You still need a strong unfairness or misuse rationale.

Then identify facts showing misuse of the entity, such as undercapitalization, commingling, failure to observe formalities, or fraud.
Ask whether respecting the corporate form would sanction unfairness or injustice.
If reverse piercing is raised, explain why courts are especially cautious because innocent creditors or shareholders may be harmed.
Conclude whether veil piercing should or should not occur on these facts.

common_traps.explanation: No single veil-piercing factor is automatically decisive. Courts usually look at the total relationship between owner and entity.

common_traps.title: Skipping injustice analysis

rules.title: Reverse Piercing Concept
rules.prompt_question: What is reverse piercing?
rules.rule_statement: Reverse piercing is the theory that a claimant may seek to reach corporate assets for the liabilities of a shareholder, though courts treat it cautiously because it may prejudice innocent shareholders or creditors.
rules.application_example: A creditor of a dominant shareholder tries to reach the shareholder's corporation's assets.
rules.common_trap: Treating one factor as enough: No single veil-piercing factor is automatically decisive. Courts usually look at the total relationship between owner and entity. Skipping injustice analysis: Undercapitalization or commingling alone is not always enough. You still need a strong unfairness or misuse rationale.
how_to_apply: Start from the presumption that the corporation is a separate legal entity.
Then identify facts showing misuse of the entity, such as undercapitalization, commingling, failure to observe formalities, or fraud.
Ask whether respecting the corporate form would sanction unfairness or injustice.
If reverse piercing is raised, explain why courts are especially cautious because innocent creditors or shareholders may be harmed.
Conclude whether veil piercing should or should not occur on these facts.

common_traps.explanation: No single veil-piercing factor is automatically decisive. Courts usually look at the total relationship between owner and entity.

common_traps.explanation: Undercapitalization or commingling alone is not always enough. You still need a strong unfairness or misuse rationale.

rules.title: Board Management Authority
rules.prompt_question: Who manages the corporation?
rules.rule_statement: The corporation's business and affairs are managed by or under the direction of the board of directors, except as otherwise provided by statute, the articles, or valid shareholder arrangements in special contexts.
rules.application_example: The board decides whether to enter a major supply agreement.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Shareholders own the corporation but generally do not manage day-to-day affairs.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the challenged act falls within board authority or whether another valid arrangement displaced the default rule.
Start with the rule that the corporation's business and affairs are managed by or under the direction of the board.
Then ask whether statute, the articles, or a valid special arrangement alters that default.
Do not confuse ownership by shareholders with management authority.
Conclude who had power to manage the corporation on these facts.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Shareholders own the corporation but generally do not manage day-to-day affairs.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate governance issues, identify the required corporate actor. Directors, officers, and shareholders have different powers, procedures, and voting rules.

rules.title: Board Meetings, Notice, and Quorum
rules.prompt_question: What are the basic rules for board meetings?
rules.rule_statement: A majority of the number of directors fixed by the articles or bylaws generally constitutes a quorum for board action, and directors typically must receive notice of special meetings unless waived. No notice is usually required for regular meetings if the time and place are fixed.
rules.application_example: A five-member board generally needs three directors for a quorum unless the governing documents provide otherwise within statutory limits.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Board action requires both a quorum and the required vote, which is usually a majority of directors present unless a greater vote is required.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the board acted at a regular meeting, special meeting, or attempted meeting without proper procedure.
Then test notice, quorum, and vote separately because each is its own requirement.
Use the facts to determine the number of directors fixed and how many were present.
Do not stop at quorum alone; ask whether the required affirmative vote was also obtained.
Conclude whether valid board action occurred.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Board action requires both a quorum and the required vote, which is usually a majority of directors present unless a greater vote is required.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate governance issues, identify the required corporate actor. Directors, officers, and shareholders have different powers, procedures, and voting rules.

rules.title: Board Action by Written Consent
rules.prompt_question: Can directors act without a meeting?
rules.rule_statement: Directors may act without a meeting if all directors consent in writing or in an electronic record, unless the governing statute provides otherwise.
rules.application_example: All directors sign a unanimous written consent approving a merger agreement.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Unlike shareholder voting, director written consent is often required to be unanimous unless a statute says otherwise.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the directors acted without a meeting by written consent.
Then determine whether the governing statute requires unanimity for board written consent.
Use the facts to see whether every director properly consented in the required form.
Do not confuse director written consent rules with shareholder written consent rules.
Conclude whether the board validly acted without a meeting.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Unlike shareholder voting, director written consent is often required to be unanimous unless a statute says otherwise.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate governance issues, identify the required corporate actor. Directors, officers, and shareholders have different powers, procedures, and voting rules.

rules.title: Board Committees
rules.prompt_question: What may a board committee do?
rules.rule_statement: The board may delegate many management functions to committees of directors, but certain fundamental decisions, such as declaring distributions or approving major structural changes, may remain reserved to the full board under statute.
rules.application_example: An audit committee oversees financial reporting, while the full board approves major mergers.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: The exact list of nondelegable matters is statutory and often tested conceptually.
how_to_apply: Identify the committee action and the power the full board attempted to delegate.
Ask whether the delegated task is one that statute allows a committee to perform.
Use the facts to distinguish ordinary delegated functions from fundamental actions reserved to the full board.
Do not assume a committee can do everything the board can do.
Conclude whether the committee action was authorized.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: The exact list of nondelegable matters is statutory and often tested conceptually.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate governance issues, identify the required corporate actor. Directors, officers, and shareholders have different powers, procedures, and voting rules.

rules.title: Director's Objection
rules.prompt_question: How may a director avoid being deemed to have assented to board action?
rules.rule_statement: A director who is present at a meeting when corporate action is taken is generally deemed to have assented unless the director objects at the beginning of the meeting or promptly upon arrival to holding it or transacting business, or the director votes against or abstains and the abstention or dissent is recorded.
rules.application_example: A director votes against an unlawful dividend and has the dissent recorded in the minutes.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Silence at the meeting may be treated as assent.
how_to_apply: Identify the board action taken while the director was present.
Then ask whether the director objected, voted against, or abstained in a manner properly recorded.
Use timing carefully because silence may count as assent.
Do not assume private disagreement is enough without a proper recorded objection or dissent.
Conclude whether the director is deemed to have assented.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Silence at the meeting may be treated as assent.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate governance issues, identify the required corporate actor. Directors, officers, and shareholders have different powers, procedures, and voting rules.

rules.title: Officer Authority
rules.prompt_question: How do officers obtain authority to bind the corporation?
rules.rule_statement: Corporate officers have actual authority as granted by the bylaws, board resolutions, or their office, and may also have apparent authority to bind the corporation in matters ordinary for their position.
rules.application_example: A corporate president usually has apparent authority to enter ordinary business contracts.
rules.common_trap: Using the agent's statements: The classic mistake is relying on the agent's own claim of authority. Apparent authority must come from the principal's manifestations to the third party. Skipping reliance: Even if the agent looked authorized, you still need reasonable third-party belief and reliance.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the officer had actual authority, apparent authority, or both.
For actual authority, look to bylaws, board resolutions, office, and prior corporate practice.
For apparent authority, focus on the corporation's manifestations to the third party, not the officer's own statements.
Then ask whether the transaction was ordinary for that officer's role.
Conclude whether the corporation is bound by the officer's act.

common_traps.title: Using the agent's statements
common_traps.explanation: The classic mistake is relying on the agent's own claim of authority. Apparent authority must come from the principal's manifestations to the third party.

common_traps.title: Skipping reliance
common_traps.explanation: Even if the agent looked authorized, you still need reasonable third-party belief and reliance.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate governance issues, identify the required corporate actor. Directors, officers, and shareholders have different powers, procedures, and voting rules.

rules.title: Officer Liability on Corporate Obligations
rules.prompt_question: When is an officer personally liable on a corporate contract?
rules.rule_statement: An officer who acts for a disclosed corporation is generally not personally liable on a corporate contract if the officer acts with authority and indicates representative capacity, unless the officer personally guarantees the obligation or lacks authority.
rules.application_example: A corporate treasurer signs a loan agreement on behalf of the corporation and is not personally liable absent a guarantee.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Signing only one's own name without indicating agency status may create ambiguity and personal liability issues.
how_to_apply: Start by determining whether the corporation was disclosed at the time of contracting.
Then ask whether the officer acted with authority and clearly indicated representative capacity.
Look closely at how the officer signed and whether any personal guarantee was given.
If authority was lacking or the signature was ambiguous, explain why personal liability may arise.
Conclude whether the officer is personally liable.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Signing only one's own name without indicating agency status may create ambiguity and personal liability issues.

rules.exam_tip: For corporate governance issues, identify the required corporate actor. Directors, officers, and shareholders have different powers, procedures, and voting rules.

rules.title: Shareholder Meetings
rules.prompt_question: What are the basic rules for annual and special shareholder meetings?
rules.rule_statement: Corporations generally must hold annual shareholder meetings, and special meetings may be called by persons authorized by statute, the articles, or bylaws. Notice must state the time and place and, for special meetings, often the purpose.
rules.application_example: The board calls the annual shareholder meeting to elect directors.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Action taken at a special meeting outside the noticed purpose may be invalid in some circumstances.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the meeting was annual or special.
Then ask whether the proper person called it and whether notice satisfied statutory and bylaw requirements.
For a special meeting, use the noticed purpose carefully and do not assume unrelated action is valid.
Separate meeting-validity questions from later voting-threshold questions.
Conclude whether the shareholder meeting was validly held.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Action taken at a special meeting outside the noticed purpose may be invalid in some circumstances.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder voting questions, separate notice, quorum, voting threshold, proxy authority, class voting, and special voting arrangements. These are different procedural requirements.

rules.title: Shareholder Quorum and Voting
rules.prompt_question: What are the default quorum and voting rules for shareholders?
rules.rule_statement: A majority of the votes entitled to be cast on a matter generally constitutes a quorum for shareholder action, and if a quorum exists, action is approved if the votes cast in favor exceed votes cast against, unless the articles, bylaws, or statute require a greater vote.
keywords: majority of votes entitled to be cast
rules.application_example: If holders of a majority of shares are present, the shareholders may vote on routine matters at the meeting.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Quorum and voting thresholds are distinct; once quorum is present, the required affirmative vote may be less than a majority of outstanding shares.
how_to_apply: Separate quorum from the vote required to pass the matter.
First ask whether the required number of shares entitled to vote was present or represented.
Then ask whether the votes cast in favor satisfied the applicable statutory, charter, or bylaw threshold.
Do not assume approval requires a majority of all outstanding shares unless the governing rule says so.
Conclude whether valid shareholder action occurred.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Quorum and voting thresholds are distinct; once quorum is present, the required affirmative vote may be less than a majority of outstanding shares.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder voting questions, separate notice, quorum, voting threshold, proxy authority, class voting, and special voting arrangements. These are different procedural requirements.

rules.title: Proxy Voting
rules.prompt_question: What is a proxy?
rules.rule_statement: A proxy is an authorization by a shareholder permitting another person to vote the shareholder's shares. Proxies are generally freely revocable unless coupled with an interest and stated to be irrevocable.
rules.application_example: A shareholder signs a proxy authorizing another person to vote at the annual meeting.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Voting trusts and proxy arrangements are distinct concepts.
how_to_apply: Identify the proxy and ask whether it validly authorized another person to vote the shareholder's shares.
Then determine whether the proxy was revocable or irrevocable under the coupled-with-an-interest rule.
Use the facts to distinguish a proxy from a voting trust.
If revocation is disputed, analyze timing and any valid irrevocability language.
Conclude who had the right to vote the shares.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Voting trusts and proxy arrangements are distinct concepts.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder voting questions, separate notice, quorum, voting threshold, proxy authority, class voting, and special voting arrangements. These are different procedural requirements.

rules.title: Voting Trusts
rules.prompt_question: What is a voting trust?
rules.rule_statement: A voting trust is an arrangement by which shareholders transfer legal title of their shares to a trustee who votes the shares pursuant to the trust agreement, while the beneficial owners retain economic interests.
rules.application_example: Founders place their shares into a voting trust to ensure unified control over corporate elections.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Voting trusts typically require formalities such as a written agreement and share transfer.
how_to_apply: Ask whether the arrangement transferred legal title of the shares to a trustee for voting purposes.
Then test the required formalities, such as a written trust arrangement and transfer of shares.
Distinguish a voting trust from a mere shareholder voting agreement or proxy.
Use the facts to separate voting control from retained economic ownership.
Conclude whether a valid voting trust exists.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Voting trusts typically require formalities such as a written agreement and share transfer.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder voting questions, separate notice, quorum, voting threshold, proxy authority, class voting, and special voting arrangements. These are different procedural requirements.

rules.title: Shareholder Voting Agreements
rules.prompt_question: Are shareholder voting agreements valid?
rules.rule_statement: Shareholder voting agreements are generally valid and specifically enforceable if they do not violate statute or public policy.
rules.application_example: Two shareholders agree in writing to vote together for each other's nominees.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Do not confuse a voting agreement with a voting trust; the former usually does not require share transfer.
how_to_apply: Identify the agreement among shareholders and ask whether it is simply a voting agreement rather than a trust or proxy.
Then ask whether it violates any statute or public policy.
Do not require transfer of shares if the arrangement is only a voting agreement.
Use the facts to determine whether the agreement is specifically enforceable.
Conclude whether the voting agreement is valid.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Do not confuse a voting agreement with a voting trust; the former usually does not require share transfer.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder voting questions, separate notice, quorum, voting threshold, proxy authority, class voting, and special voting arrangements. These are different procedural requirements.

rules.title: Cumulative Voting
rules.prompt_question: What is cumulative voting?
rules.rule_statement: Cumulative voting allows shareholders in director elections to multiply the number of shares owned by the number of directors to be elected and cast the resulting votes for one or more candidates, if authorized by the corporation's governing law or charter.
rules.application_example: A minority shareholder pools votes to elect at least one board member under cumulative voting.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Cumulative voting is not universal; many corporations do not provide it absent charter authorization or statute.
how_to_apply: First ask whether cumulative voting is authorized by the governing statute or charter.
Then calculate the available votes by multiplying the shares owned by the number of directors to be elected.
Use the facts to determine whether the shareholder may concentrate votes on one or more candidates.
Do not assume cumulative voting exists by default in every corporation.
Conclude whether the voting method is valid and what result it allows.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Cumulative voting is not universal; many corporations do not provide it absent charter authorization or statute.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder voting questions, separate notice, quorum, voting threshold, proxy authority, class voting, and special voting arrangements. These are different procedural requirements.

rules.title: Class Voting
rules.prompt_question: When is class voting required?
rules.rule_statement: Certain corporate actions that affect a class of shares differently, such as amendments altering class rights, may require separate class voting by that class in addition to any overall shareholder vote.
rules.application_example: An amendment reducing dividend rights of preferred stock may require approval by the preferred class.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Class voting focuses on direct legal effect on the class, not merely economic consequences shared by all shareholders.
how_to_apply: Identify the corporate action and the share class allegedly affected.
Ask whether the action directly changes legal rights of that class rather than merely causing indirect economic effects.
Then determine whether separate class voting is required in addition to any overall shareholder vote.
Use the facts to identify which class must approve the action.
Conclude whether valid class approval occurred.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Class voting focuses on direct legal effect on the class, not merely economic consequences shared by all shareholders.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder voting questions, separate notice, quorum, voting threshold, proxy authority, class voting, and special voting arrangements. These are different procedural requirements.

rules.subtopic: Close Corporations and Special Control Devices
rules.title: Close Corporation Agreements
rules.prompt_question: What special agreements are common in close corporations?
rules.rule_statement: Close corporation shareholders may adopt agreements restricting share transfer, allocating management powers among shareholders, resolving deadlocks, or creating buy-sell rights, and such agreements are often enforceable if consistent with statute.
rules.application_example: A close corporation agreement gives shareholders the right of first refusal before any share sale to outsiders.
rules.common_trap: Analyzing like a public company: Close corporation disputes are often treated more like partnership disputes, especially when minority owners reasonably expected employment, management participation, or distributions. Ignoring equitable remedies: The exam may want a buyout, dissolution, or another equitable solution, not just damages.
how_to_apply: Identify the shareholder agreement and the specific close-corporation control device involved.
Ask whether the agreement reallocates management power, restricts transfer, resolves deadlock, or creates buy-sell rights.
Then test whether the agreement is consistent with statute and enforceable in the close-corporation setting.
Do not analyze the problem like a widely held public corporation if the facts show a close corporation.
Conclude whether the special agreement is valid and what effect it has.

common_traps.title: Analyzing like a public company
common_traps.explanation: Close corporation disputes are often treated more like partnership disputes, especially when minority owners reasonably expected employment, management participation, or distributions.

common_traps.title: Ignoring equitable remedies
common_traps.explanation: The exam may want a buyout, dissolution, or another equitable solution, not just damages.

rules.exam_tip: For close corporation questions, do not analyze the dispute like a public-company problem. Look for reasonable expectations, freeze-out tactics, oppression, and equitable remedies.

rules.title: Director Duty of Care
rules.prompt_question: What is the director's duty of care?
rules.rule_statement: A director must act in good faith, with the care that a person in a like position would reasonably believe appropriate under similar circumstances, and in a manner the director reasonably believes to be in the best interests of the corporation.
rules.application_example: Directors reviewing a merger should inform themselves and deliberate before approving the transaction.
rules.common_trap: Outcome bias: A bad business result does not itself prove a breach. The real issue is whether the directors were informed, disinterested, and acting in good faith. Skipping process: Students often argue fairness of the result without analyzing the board's decision-making process.
how_to_apply: Identify the board decision being challenged and the decision-making process used.
Ask whether the directors acted in good faith, were adequately informed, and reasonably believed they were serving the corporation's best interests.
If the process was informed and disinterested, explain why the business judgment rule may protect the decision.
If the process was uninformed, conflicted, or in bad faith, explain why the presumption may be lost.
Conclude whether liability should attach or whether the decision is protected from judicial second-guessing.

common_traps.title: Outcome bias
common_traps.explanation: A bad business result does not itself prove a breach. The real issue is whether the directors were informed, disinterested, and acting in good faith.

common_traps.title: Skipping process
common_traps.explanation: Students often argue fairness of the result without analyzing the board's decision-making process.

rules.exam_tip: For duty of care and business judgment rule questions, emphasize process: informed decision-making, good faith, independence, and absence of conflict. A bad result alone is not enough.

rules.title: Business Judgment Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the business judgment rule?
rules.rule_statement: The business judgment rule creates a presumption that directors acted on an informed basis, in good faith, and with the honest belief that the action taken was in the corporation's best interests. Courts generally will not second-guess such decisions absent fraud, illegality, conflict of interest, or gross abuse.
rules.application_example: A board's good-faith decision to expand into a new market is usually protected even if the venture later fails.
rules.common_trap: Outcome bias: A bad business result does not itself prove a breach. The real issue is whether the directors were informed, disinterested, and acting in good faith. Skipping process: Students often argue fairness of the result without analyzing the board's decision-making process.
how_to_apply: Identify the board decision being challenged and the decision-making process used.
Ask whether the directors acted in good faith, were adequately informed, and reasonably believed they were serving the corporation's best interests.
If the process was informed and disinterested, explain why the business judgment rule may protect the decision.
If the process was uninformed, conflicted, or in bad faith, explain why the presumption may be lost.
Conclude whether liability should attach or whether the decision is protected from judicial second-guessing.

common_traps.title: Outcome bias
common_traps.explanation: A bad business result does not itself prove a breach. The real issue is whether the directors were informed, disinterested, and acting in good faith.

common_traps.title: Skipping process
common_traps.explanation: Students often argue fairness of the result without analyzing the board's decision-making process.

rules.exam_tip: For duty of care and business judgment rule questions, emphasize process: informed decision-making, good faith, independence, and absence of conflict. A bad result alone is not enough.

rules.title: Director Duty of Loyalty
rules.prompt_question: What is the director's duty of loyalty?
rules.rule_statement: A director owes the corporation a duty of loyalty and must not engage in self-dealing, usurp corporate opportunities, compete unfairly with the corporation, or otherwise place personal interests above the corporation's interests.
rules.application_example: A director cannot secretly acquire for personal benefit a business opportunity the corporation was considering.
rules.common_trap: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company. Forgetting cleansing mechanisms: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.
how_to_apply: Start by identifying the personal interest, conflict, opportunity, or control abuse at issue.
Ask whether the fiduciary put personal interests ahead of the corporation or minority owners.
Then analyze cleansing facts such as full disclosure, approval by disinterested decision-makers, fairness, or valid rejection by the corporation.
Do not use the business judgment rule as a shortcut in a loyalty problem.
Conclude whether the fiduciary duty was breached and what remedy may follow.

common_traps.title: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong
common_traps.explanation: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company.

common_traps.title: Forgetting cleansing mechanisms
common_traps.explanation: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.

rules.exam_tip: For duty of loyalty questions, look for conflict, self-dealing, personal benefit, or usurped opportunity. Then analyze disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation.

rules.title: Director Conflict-of-Interest Transaction
rules.prompt_question: When is a director conflict-of-interest transaction valid?
rules.rule_statement: A director's conflicting-interest transaction is generally not automatically void if it is disclosed and approved by disinterested directors or shareholders, or if the transaction was fair to the corporation at the time it was entered.
rules.application_example: A corporation leases office space from a director after full disclosure and approval by disinterested directors.
rules.common_trap: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company. Forgetting cleansing mechanisms: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.
how_to_apply: Start by identifying the personal interest, conflict, opportunity, or control abuse at issue.
Ask whether the fiduciary put personal interests ahead of the corporation or minority owners.
Then analyze cleansing facts such as full disclosure, approval by disinterested decision-makers, fairness, or valid rejection by the corporation.
Do not use the business judgment rule as a shortcut in a loyalty problem.
Conclude whether the fiduciary duty was breached and what remedy may follow.

rules.title: Corporate Opportunity Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: What is the corporate opportunity doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: Directors and officers may not take for themselves a business opportunity that is within the corporation's line of business, that the corporation has an interest or expectancy in, or that the corporation is financially able to undertake, unless the opportunity is first offered to the corporation and properly rejected.
rules.application_example: A director learns of land ideal for corporate expansion and may not buy it personally without first offering it to the corporation.
rules.common_trap: Using the business judgment rule where it does not belong: Duty-of-loyalty problems are not protected just because the fiduciary says the deal was good for the company. Forgetting cleansing mechanisms: Students often ignore disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation, all of which may change the outcome.

Ask whether the fiduciary put personal interests ahead of the corporation or minority owners.
Then analyze cleansing facts such as full disclosure, approval by disinterested decision-makers, fairness, or valid rejection by the corporation.
Do not use the business judgment rule as a shortcut in a loyalty problem.

rules.title: Officer Fiduciary Duties
rules.prompt_question: Do officers owe fiduciary duties?
rules.rule_statement: Corporate officers owe fiduciary duties of care and loyalty similar to those owed by directors, adapted to their role and authority within the corporation.
rules.application_example: A chief financial officer may be liable for self-dealing or grossly negligent financial oversight.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Because officers act operationally, agency authority issues often overlap with fiduciary-duty issues.
how_to_apply: Start by identifying the personal interest, conflict, opportunity, or control abuse at issue.
Ask whether the fiduciary put personal interests ahead of the corporation or minority owners.
Then analyze cleansing facts such as full disclosure, approval by disinterested decision-makers, fairness, or valid rejection by the corporation.
Do not use the business judgment rule as a shortcut in a loyalty problem.
Conclude whether the fiduciary duty was breached and what remedy may follow.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Because officers act operationally, agency authority issues often overlap with fiduciary-duty issues.

rules.exam_tip: For duty of loyalty questions, look for conflict, self-dealing, personal benefit, or usurped opportunity. Then analyze disclosure, disinterested approval, fairness, or rejection by the corporation.

rules.title: Controlling Shareholder Duties
rules.prompt_question: When may a controlling shareholder owe fiduciary duties?
rules.rule_statement: A controlling shareholder may owe fiduciary duties to minority shareholders and the corporation when exercising control, particularly in freeze-outs, self-dealing transactions, or oppressive conduct in close corporations.
rules.application_example: A majority shareholder causes the corporation to enter a transaction benefiting the majority at the minority's expense.
rules.common_trap: Analyzing like a public company: Close corporation disputes are often treated more like partnership disputes, especially when minority owners reasonably expected employment, management participation, or distributions. Ignoring equitable remedies: The exam may want a buyout, dissolution, or another equitable solution, not just damages.
how_to_apply: Start by identifying the personal interest, conflict, opportunity, or control abuse at issue.
Ask whether the fiduciary put personal interests ahead of the corporation or minority owners.
Then analyze cleansing facts such as full disclosure, approval by disinterested decision-makers, fairness, or valid rejection by the corporation.
Do not use the business judgment rule as a shortcut in a loyalty problem.
Conclude whether the fiduciary duty was breached and what remedy may follow.

rules.subtopic: Close Corporations and Special Control Devices
rules.title: Minority Oppression in Close Corporations
rules.prompt_question: What is minority oppression in a close corporation?
rules.rule_statement: Minority oppression occurs when controlling shareholders in a close corporation frustrate the reasonable expectations of minority owners through exclusion from management, withholding distributions, termination of employment, or other unfair conduct. Courts may grant equitable relief, including buyouts or dissolution.
rules.application_example: Majority shareholders freeze out a minority shareholder by firing the shareholder from the family business and stopping all dividends.
rules.common_trap: Analyzing like a public company: Close corporation disputes are often treated more like partnership disputes, especially when minority owners reasonably expected employment, management participation, or distributions. Ignoring equitable remedies: The exam may want a buyout, dissolution, or another equitable solution, not just damages.
how_to_apply: Identify the conduct allegedly frustrating the minority owner's reasonable expectations in the close corporation.
Use the facts to test exclusion from management, withholding distributions, termination of employment, or other freeze-out tactics.
Do not analyze the dispute like an ordinary public-company governance matter.
Then ask what equitable remedy fits, such as buyout, dissolution, or another court-ordered solution.
Conclude whether minority oppression occurred.

common_traps.title: Analyzing like a public company
common_traps.explanation: Close corporation disputes are often treated more like partnership disputes, especially when minority owners reasonably expected employment, management participation, or distributions.

common_traps.title: Ignoring equitable remedies
common_traps.explanation: The exam may want a buyout, dissolution, or another equitable solution, not just damages.

rules.exam_tip: For close corporation questions, do not analyze the dispute like a public-company problem. Look for reasonable expectations, freeze-out tactics, oppression, and equitable remedies.

rules.title: Distributions to Shareholders
rules.prompt_question: When may a corporation make distributions?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation may make distributions to shareholders unless, after giving effect to the distribution, the corporation would be unable to pay its debts as they become due in the usual course of business or its total assets would be less than the sum of its total liabilities and any preferential amounts due to superior shares on dissolution.
keywords: unable to pay debts as they become due
rules.application_example: A board may declare a dividend only if the corporation remains solvent under statutory tests.
rules.common_trap: Ignoring solvency tests: Students often discuss dividends as if the board can simply choose to pay them. The legal issue is whether the corporation remains solvent under the statute. Forgetting director liability: If the distribution is unlawful, the next issue is often whether directors who approved it breached the required standard of conduct.
how_to_apply: Identify the proposed distribution and start with the statutory solvency limits.
Apply both the inability-to-pay-debts test and the balance-sheet style test, if both are relevant under the statute.
Use the facts after giving effect to the distribution, not before it.
Do not assume the board may pay dividends just because it wants to.
Conclude whether the corporation may lawfully make the distribution.

common_traps.title: Ignoring solvency tests
common_traps.explanation: Students often discuss dividends as if the board can simply choose to pay them. The legal issue is whether the corporation remains solvent under the statute.

common_traps.title: Forgetting director liability
common_traps.explanation: If the distribution is unlawful, the next issue is often whether directors who approved it breached the required standard of conduct.

rules.exam_tip: For distributions, apply the solvency limits after giving effect to the distribution. If the distribution is unlawful, analyze director liability next.

rules.title: Director Liability for Unlawful Distribution
rules.prompt_question: When are directors liable for unlawful distributions?
rules.rule_statement: Directors who vote for or assent to an unlawful distribution may be personally liable to the corporation for the amount exceeding what could have been properly distributed if they failed to comply with the applicable standard of conduct.
rules.application_example: Directors approve a dividend that leaves the corporation insolvent and may be liable for resulting loss.
rules.common_trap: Ignoring solvency tests: Students often discuss dividends as if the board can simply choose to pay them. The legal issue is whether the corporation remains solvent under the statute. Forgetting director liability: If the distribution is unlawful, the next issue is often whether directors who approved it breached the required standard of conduct.
how_to_apply: Start by asking whether the distribution itself was unlawful under the governing solvency rules.
Then identify which directors voted for, assented to, or failed properly to object to the distribution.
Ask whether those directors complied with the required standard of conduct.
Separate liability to the corporation from any contribution or recourse among directors or shareholders.
Conclude whether the directors face liability for the unlawful distribution.

common_traps.title: Ignoring solvency tests
common_traps.explanation: Students often discuss dividends as if the board can simply choose to pay them. The legal issue is whether the corporation remains solvent under the statute.

common_traps.title: Forgetting director liability
common_traps.explanation: If the distribution is unlawful, the next issue is often whether directors who approved it breached the required standard of conduct.

rules.exam_tip: For distributions, apply the solvency limits after giving effect to the distribution. If the distribution is unlawful, analyze director liability next.

rules.title: Authorized Shares and Issuance
rules.prompt_question: What is the significance of authorized shares?
rules.rule_statement: The articles fix the number of shares the corporation is authorized to issue. The board may issue shares up to that number in exchange for consideration approved by the board, subject to statutory requirements.
rules.application_example: A corporation authorized for one million shares may issue only up to that amount unless the articles are amended.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Issuing shares beyond the authorized number creates overissue problems.
how_to_apply: Identify the number of shares authorized in the articles and the number the board attempted to issue.
Ask whether the issuance stays within the authorized amount.
Then analyze whether the board approved valid consideration for the issuance.
Do not ignore charter limits on the corporation's power to issue stock.
Conclude whether the share issuance was valid.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Issuing shares beyond the authorized number creates overissue problems.

rules.exam_tip: For share-issuance and financing issues, check the articles, board approval, statutory limits, and whether shareholder rights such as preemptive rights are actually provided.

rules.title: Preemptive Rights
rules.prompt_question: What are preemptive rights?
rules.rule_statement: Preemptive rights give existing shareholders the right to maintain their proportional ownership by purchasing a proportionate share of certain new issuances, but such rights generally exist only if granted by the articles or governing statute.
rules.application_example: A charter grants shareholders the right to buy a pro rata portion of newly issued common stock.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Modern statutes often do not grant preemptive rights by default.
how_to_apply: Start by asking whether preemptive rights exist under the articles or governing statute.
If they do, identify whether the challenged issuance is the kind of issuance that triggers those rights.
Use the facts to determine the shareholder's proportional entitlement to purchase new shares.
Do not assume preemptive rights exist by default unless the governing rule provides them.
Conclude whether the shareholder had enforceable preemptive rights.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Modern statutes often do not grant preemptive rights by default.

rules.exam_tip: For share-issuance and financing issues, check the articles, board approval, statutory limits, and whether shareholder rights such as preemptive rights are actually provided.

rules.title: Treasury Shares
rules.prompt_question: What are treasury shares?
rules.rule_statement: Treasury shares are shares previously issued and later reacquired by the corporation. They are generally treated as authorized but unissued shares unless the articles provide otherwise.
rules.application_example: A corporation buys back some of its shares and later reissues them as treasury shares.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Treasury shares usually do not carry voting or dividend rights while held by the corporation.
how_to_apply: Identify whether the corporation reacquired previously issued shares.
Then classify those shares as treasury shares or the equivalent under the governing statute.
Explain their status while held by the corporation, including voting and dividend consequences.
Use the facts to determine whether later reissuance is permitted.
Conclude the legal status and effect of the treasury shares.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: Treasury shares usually do not carry voting or dividend rights while held by the corporation.

rules.exam_tip: For share-issuance and financing issues, check the articles, board approval, statutory limits, and whether shareholder rights such as preemptive rights are actually provided.

rules.title: Inspection Rights
rules.prompt_question: What records may shareholders inspect?
rules.rule_statement: Shareholders generally have the right to inspect certain corporate records, such as articles, bylaws, minutes, and accounting records, if they comply with statutory procedures and, for some records, show a proper purpose.
rules.application_example: A shareholder seeks inspection of accounting records to investigate suspected mismanagement.
rules.common_trap: Forgetting proper purpose: Not all corporate records are equally available. Many requests require compliance with statutory procedure and a proper purpose. Treating all records the same: Basic records and sensitive records often have different inspection standards.
how_to_apply: Identify the records the shareholder wants to inspect and whether they are basic records or more sensitive records.
Then ask whether the shareholder complied with statutory procedure.
If the records require a proper purpose, explain the stated purpose and whether it is reasonably related to shareholder interests.
Do not treat all categories of records as equally available on the same terms.
Conclude whether inspection must be allowed.

common_traps.title: Forgetting proper purpose
common_traps.explanation: Not all corporate records are equally available. Many requests require compliance with statutory procedure and a proper purpose.

common_traps.title: Treating all records the same
common_traps.explanation: Basic records and sensitive records often have different inspection standards.

rules.exam_tip: For inspection rights, identify the type of record requested. Basic records and sensitive records often have different standards, and proper purpose may be required.

rules.title: Direct Action
rules.prompt_question: When may a shareholder bring a direct action?
rules.rule_statement: A shareholder may bring a direct action for injuries suffered individually, such as denial of voting rights, denial of inspection rights, or oppression, rather than injuries suffered by the corporation as a whole.
rules.application_example: A shareholder sues directly after being denied the right to vote shares at a meeting.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: If the injury is primarily to the corporation, the proper action is derivative rather than direct.
how_to_apply: Start by classifying the injury as personal to the shareholder or belonging to the corporation.
If the injury is individual, explain why the claim may be brought directly rather than derivatively.
Use the facts to distinguish denial of voting, inspection, or oppression rights from generalized corporate injury.
Do not force a derivative framework onto an individual-rights claim.
Conclude whether the shareholder may proceed directly.

common_traps.title: Common Trap
common_traps.explanation: If the injury is primarily to the corporation, the proper action is derivative rather than direct.

rules.exam_tip: For shareholder litigation, classify the claim first. Direct claims protect individual shareholder rights; derivative claims belong to the corporation and require procedural analysis.

rules.title: Derivative Action
rules.prompt_question: What is a derivative action?
rules.rule_statement: A derivative action is a suit brought by a shareholder on behalf of the corporation to enforce a corporate cause of action when those in control of the corporation fail to do so.
rules.application_example: A shareholder sues directors derivatively for waste of corporate assets.
rules.common_trap: Choosing the wrong claim type: Students often call a corporate injury a direct claim when it really belongs to the corporation and must be brought derivatively. Ignoring procedure: Derivative claims are often lost on demand, standing, contemporaneous ownership, or committee-review issues rather than the merits.
how_to_apply: Begin by classifying the claim as derivative because the injury belongs to the corporation.
Then test the procedural requirements such as contemporaneous ownership, adequate representation, and demand rules.
If demand or committee review is involved, analyze independence, good faith, and procedural sufficiency carefully.
Do not skip the procedural hurdles and jump straight to the merits.
Conclude whether the derivative action may proceed.

common_traps.title: Choosing the wrong claim type

rules.title: Derivative Action Requirements
rules.prompt_question: What must a shareholder generally show to bring a derivative suit?
rules.rule_statement: A derivative plaintiff generally must have been a shareholder at the time of the challenged act or have acquired shares by operation of law, must fairly and adequately represent the corporation's interests, and must make a written demand on the corporation unless demand is excused where recognized by law.
rules.application_example: A current shareholder who owned stock when the alleged self-dealing occurred makes a written demand on the board before suing.

rules.rule_statement: Before filing a derivative action, a shareholder usually must make a written demand requesting that the corporation bring the action, and then wait the required statutory period unless irreparable injury would result or demand is excused under applicable law.
rules.application_example: A shareholder sends a formal demand letter asking the board to sue a director for self-dealing.

rules.title: Shareholder Class Actions
rules.prompt_question: When might shareholders proceed by class action instead of derivative suit?
rules.rule_statement: A shareholder class action may be appropriate when shareholders suffer a common direct injury, such as misleading proxy disclosures affecting voting or transactions impacting shareholders in the same direct manner.
rules.application_example: Shareholders sue as a class for inadequate disclosures in a merger vote.
rules.common_trap: Common Trap: Derivative claims belong to the corporation and are not transformed into class claims merely because many shareholders are affected indirectly.

rules.title: LLC Formation
rules.prompt_question: How is an LLC formed?
rules.rule_statement: A limited liability company is formed by filing articles of organization or a certificate of formation with the state, creating a separate legal entity distinct from its members.
rules.application_example: Entrepreneurs file a certificate of formation to create an LLC for a startup venture.
rules.common_trap: Importing corporate rules automatically: LLC problems often turn on the operating agreement and statutory defaults, not on corporate law by analogy. Ignoring management structure: Authority analysis changes depending on whether the LLC is member-managed or manager-managed.
how_to_apply: Start with statutory filing because an LLC is created by filing the certificate or articles required by statute.
Then identify the LLC as a separate legal entity distinct from its members.
Do not import corporation or partnership formation rules automatically.
Use the facts to determine whether the required filing actually occurred and when the entity came into existence.
Conclude whether a valid LLC was formed.

common_traps.title: Importing corporate rules automatically
common_traps.explanation: LLC problems often turn on the operating agreement and statutory defaults, not on corporate law by analogy.

common_traps.title: Ignoring management structure
common_traps.explanation: Authority analysis changes depending on whether the LLC is member-managed or manager-managed.

rules.exam_tip: For LLC questions, start with the operating agreement and management structure. Then apply statutory defaults only where the agreement is silent or limited by nonwaivable law.

rules.title: Operating Agreement
rules.prompt_question: What is an LLC operating agreement?
rules.rule_statement: An operating agreement governs the relations among the members and managers and between them and the LLC, including management structure, allocations, distributions, transfer restrictions, and dissolution rights, subject to nonwaivable statutory limits.
rules.application_example: An operating agreement provides that one member manages daily operations while profits are split 60-40.
rules.common_trap: Importing corporate rules automatically: LLC problems often turn on the operating agreement and statutory defaults, not on corporate law by analogy. Ignoring management structure: Authority analysis changes depending on whether the LLC is member-managed or manager-managed.
how_to_apply: Identify the operating agreement provision at issue and what subject it governs.
Then ask whether the operating agreement controls that issue or whether a nonwaivable statutory limit overrides it.
Use the facts to determine management structure, allocation, transfer, or dissolution consequences under the agreement.
Do not default to corporate analogies before checking the agreement.
Conclude what rights and duties the operating agreement creates.
