rules.title: Member-Managed LLC
rules.prompt_question: What is a member-managed LLC?
rules.rule_statement: In a member-managed LLC, each member has authority to participate in management, and each member is generally an agent of the LLC for matters in the ordinary course of the LLC's activities.
rules.application_example: In a small LLC with no managers, each member can bind the LLC in ordinary transactions.
rules.common_trap: Importing corporate rules automatically: LLC problems often turn on the operating agreement and statutory defaults, not on corporate law by analogy. Ignoring management structure: Authority analysis changes depending on whether the LLC is member-managed or manager-managed.
how_to_apply: Start by confirming that the LLC is member-managed rather than manager-managed.
Then apply the rule that each member generally participates in management and may bind the LLC in the ordinary course.
Ask whether the challenged act was ordinary or extraordinary for the LLC's business.
Use the facts to determine whether the third party had notice of any lack of authority.
Conclude whether the member had authority to bind the LLC.

rules.title: Manager-Managed LLC
rules.prompt_question: What is a manager-managed LLC?
rules.rule_statement: In a manager-managed LLC, management authority is vested in one or more managers rather than the members, and ordinary management powers belong to the managers, while members usually act more like passive owners unless the operating agreement provides otherwise.
rules.application_example: An LLC appoints one professional manager to run operations while outside investors remain members only.
rules.common_trap: Importing corporate rules automatically: LLC problems often turn on the operating agreement and statutory defaults, not on corporate law by analogy. Ignoring management structure: Authority analysis changes depending on whether the LLC is member-managed or manager-managed.
how_to_apply: Start by confirming that the LLC is manager-managed.
Then explain that ordinary management authority belongs to the managers rather than the members.
Use the operating agreement and statutory defaults to determine whether the challenged actor had power to act.
Do not analyze passive members as if they automatically held ordinary management authority.
Conclude who had management power on these facts.

rules.title: Member Liability
rules.prompt_question: Are LLC members personally liable for LLC obligations?
rules.rule_statement: LLC members are generally not personally liable for the debts, obligations, or liabilities of the LLC solely by reason of being members or participating in management.
rules.application_example: A creditor of the LLC ordinarily cannot collect the LLC's contract debt from a member personally.
rules.common_trap: Importing corporate rules automatically: LLC problems often turn on the operating agreement and statutory defaults, not on corporate law by analogy. Ignoring management structure: Authority analysis changes depending on whether the LLC is member-managed or manager-managed.
how_to_apply: Begin with the rule that LLC members are generally not personally liable for LLC obligations solely because of membership or management participation.
Then separate entity debts from personal guarantees, personal torts, or veil-piercing facts.
Do not import general-partnership personal-liability rules into an LLC problem.
Use the facts to identify whether any separate basis for personal liability exists.
Conclude whether the member is personally liable.

rules.title: Manager Authority
rules.prompt_question: How do managers bind the LLC?
rules.rule_statement: Managers in a manager-managed LLC have actual and often apparent authority to bind the LLC in the ordinary course of the LLC's business, subject to limitations in the operating agreement and statutory notice rules.
rules.application_example: An LLC manager signs a routine vendor agreement for the company.

Apparent authority must come from the principal's manifestations to the third party. Skipping reliance: Even if the agent looked authorized, you still need reasonable third-party belief and reliance.

Ask whether the manager had actual authority under the operating agreement or statutory defaults.

Use the facts to determine whether the act was ordinary for the LLC and whether the third party reasonably relied.
Conclude whether the LLC is bound.

rules.title: LLC Fiduciary Duties
rules.prompt_question: What fiduciary duties may members and managers owe in an LLC?
rules.rule_statement: Depending on the statute and operating agreement, members in a member-managed LLC and managers in a manager-managed LLC generally owe duties of loyalty and care to the LLC and, in some circumstances, to the other members, though the operating agreement may modify these duties within statutory bounds.

rules.title: Transfer of LLC Interests
rules.prompt_question: What may an LLC member transfer?
rules.rule_statement: Absent agreement otherwise, a member may transfer the member's transferable interest, which generally consists of economic rights to distributions, but the transferee does not become a member or obtain management rights without the consent required by statute or the operating agreement.
rules.application_example: A member assigns the right to receive future LLC distributions to a creditor, but the creditor cannot vote or manage the LLC.

Then apply the rule that a transferee usually gets distribution rights, not management rights, absent required consent.

rules.title: LLC Dissociation and Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: How do dissociation and dissolution work in an LLC?
rules.rule_statement: A member may dissociate under the statute or operating agreement, and dissolution may occur upon events specified in the operating agreement, member consent, expiration of duration, judicial decree, or other statutory grounds, followed by winding up of LLC affairs.
rules.application_example: Members vote to dissolve the LLC and liquidate its remaining assets.

rules.title: Mergers and Consolidations
rules.prompt_question: What approvals are generally required for a merger?
rules.rule_statement: A merger generally requires board approval and shareholder approval by the requisite vote of each constituent corporation, unless a statutory short-form merger or other exception applies. The surviving entity succeeds to the rights and liabilities of the constituents by operation of law.
rules.application_example: Two corporations merge after both boards approve and their shareholders vote in favor.
rules.common_trap: Skipping approval or trigger analysis: Students often jump to the result without first identifying what event occurred and what approval, filing, or statutory trigger was actually required. Blending distinct structural steps: Keep separate issues separate. Do not blend dissociation with dissolution, or board approval with shareholder approval, or winding up with ongoing business operations.
how_to_apply: Identify the merger and the constituent entities involved.
Ask what approvals were required from the board, shareholders, and any affected share classes.
Use the facts to test whether statutory merger procedures and filings were completed.
Then explain that the surviving entity succeeds to rights and liabilities by operation of law.
Conclude whether the merger was valid and what legal effect it has.

rules.title: Sale of Substantially All Assets
rules.prompt_question: What approvals are generally required for a sale of substantially all assets?
rules.rule_statement: A sale, lease, exchange, or other disposition of all or substantially all corporate assets outside the usual and regular course of business generally requires board approval and shareholder approval, while ordinary-course dispositions usually do not.

rules.title: Amending Articles

rules.title: Corporate Dissolution
rules.prompt_question: How is a corporation voluntarily dissolved?
rules.rule_statement: A corporation may generally dissolve voluntarily by board action followed by shareholder approval and filing articles of dissolution, after which the corporation continues only to wind up affairs, pay creditors, and distribute remaining assets.

rules.title: Parent-Subsidiary Structure
rules.prompt_question: What is the basic legal relationship between a parent and subsidiary?
rules.rule_statement: A parent corporation that owns a controlling interest in a subsidiary does not automatically become liable for the subsidiary's obligations merely by ownership; each corporation remains a separate legal entity absent veil-piercing, assumption of liability, or another exception.
rules.application_example: A parent company generally is not liable for a subsidiary's contract debts solely because it owns the subsidiary's shares.

Then ask whether some exception such as veil piercing, assumption of liability, or another recognized doctrine applies.
Do not treat stock ownership alone as enough to impose liability.
Use the facts to determine whether the parent actually misused the subsidiary form or separately assumed liability.
Conclude whether the parent is liable for the subsidiary's obligation.
